findings.title: GlobalKey child wrapped in place within a multi-child parent: stolen element is deactivated out of its new home, State disposed, and the next rebuild crashes
findings.failure_scenario: Frame 1: Row(children: [KeyedW(key: gk), Text('b')]). Frame 2: Row(children: [Text('a'), WrapB(child: KeyedW(key: gk))]) — the keyed child stays under the same Row but gains a wrapper. During Row's _reconcileChildren, inflating WrapB transitively re-inflates KeyedW(gk), which steals the old element via _retakeInactiveElement (forgetChild removes it from Row._children) and reactivates it under WrapB. But the reconcile's local keyedOlds map still holds it, so the leftover cleanup loop deactivates the just-relocated element: detaches its render object, nulls _parent, and puts it in _inactiveElements — while WrapB's _child still references it. At flush end it is finalized: State.dispose runs (GlobalKey state destroyed despite the documented 'relocated intact' contract; deactivate() also fired twice). On the NEXT rebuild of the Row (any inherited/theme change, resize, etc.), _syncChildRenderObjects -> _findFirstRenderObject walks WrapB's dangling defunct child and the renderObject getter throws 'Bad state: SingleChildRenderObjectElement has no render object (not mounted)'. Verified with a runnable repro in BOTH assert and release modes: log shows deactivate x2, dispose, then the StateError on the following updateRoot.
findings.evidence: packages/fleury/lib/src/widgets/framework.dart:1896-1898 — leftover cleanup `for (final el in keyedOlds.values) { deactivateOld(el); }` runs after the per-slot loop; keyedOlds was snapshotted at :1827-1848 before any inflate, and a mid-loop steal via _retakeInactiveElement (:943-974) only calls forgetChild (:2001-2003, rebuilds _children) — nothing removes the stolen element from keyedOlds (Flutter consults a _forgottenChildren set here; Fleury has no equivalent). deactivateOld -> _deactivateChild (:589-611) then rips the element from its new parent (detachRenderObject walks its NEW parent chain, _parent=null) while the thief's _child pointer (ComponentElement._child) is never cleared. Crash site: _findFirstRenderObject :1984-1991 has no defunct guard, renderObject getter throws at :1634-1640.

v1.why: Reproduced at HEAD. Chain: Row._reconcileChildren slot-1 inflateWidget(WrapB) -> build -> inflateWidget(KeyedW) -> _retakeInactiveElement (framework.dart:943) -> Row.forgetChild (:2001) rebuilds _children but leaves the stolen element in the local keyedOlds map (:1811); leftover loop :1896 then _deactivateChild's the just-relocated element while WrapB._child still references it; _finalizeInactiveElements (:1444) disposes its State. Repro log: deactivate x2, activate, dispose, gk.currentContext null, pump1 succeeds silently; next Row rebuild threw 'Bad state: SingleChildRenderObjectElement has no render object (not mounted)' (:1637); tester teardown then double-unmounts ('Element has no owner').
v1.fix_sketch: Mirror Flutter's _forgottenChildren: have MultiChildRenderObjectElement.forgetChild record into a per-reconcile forgotten set (or re-check identical(el._parent, this)) consulted by both leftover-cleanup loops and the keyed-match path before deactivating/matching. Same fix covers finding 3.

findings.title: Reclaiming a GlobalKey element nested inside an already-deactivated subtree double-deactivates it: assert crash in debug; in release the moved subtree goes permanently deaf to InheritedWidget/external dependencies
findings.failure_scenario: Frame 1: Row(children: [WrapA(child: KeyedW(key: gk)), Text('b')]); frame 2: Row(children: [Text('a'), WrapB(child: KeyedW(key: gk))]). Slot 0 reconciles first: WrapA's subtree is deactivated (keyed element becomes inactive while still parented inside it). Slot 1 then inflates WrapB, whose build reclaims gk: _retakeInactiveElement sees element._parent != null and calls parent._deactivateChild(element), which unconditionally re-runs _deactivateRecursively on the already-inactive subtree. Debug (dart test / --enable-asserts): AssertionError at framework.dart:625 ''_lifecycle == _ElementLifecycle.active'' — the frame fails and the fail-closed catch in _deactivateChild unmounts the subtree, destroying the GlobalKey State (verified: repro test fails with exactly this assert). Release: State.deactivate()/Element.deactivate() hooks fire TWICE (paired service unregistration runs twice), and the second _deactivate recomputes _hadDependenciesWhenDeactivated from the now-empty dependency sets, setting it false — so _activate skips the forced markNeedsBuild that re-registers dependencies. Descendants whose widget instances are identical (const/cached children) never rebuild and never re-register: verified repro shows a child reading an InheritedWidget keeps rendering value 1 forever after the value changes to 2 (stale UI, no recovery).
findings.evidence: packages/fleury/lib/src/widgets/framework.dart:596 — _deactivateChild calls child._deactivateRecursively() unconditionally; :625 assert(_lifecycle == _ElementLifecycle.active) fails for the nested-inactive case reached from _retakeInactiveElement :956-958 (parent non-null for elements nested inside a deactivated subtree — only the subtree ROOT gets _parent nulled at :594). Release consequence: :626-627 overwrites _hadDependenciesWhenDeactivated with false (deps already cleared by the first deactivation), defeating the re-registration forced at :670-673; :631 runs the deactivate() hook a second time. Flutter's counterpart guards with `if (element._active) _deactivateRecursively(element)` in _InactiveElements.add; Fleury has no such guard.

v1.why: Reproduced at HEAD. Slot 0 deactivateOld(WrapA) nulls only the subtree ROOT's _parent (:594), so the nested keyed element stays inactive with _parent set; slot 1's inflate -> _retakeInactiveElement :956-958 calls parent._deactivateChild(element) -> _deactivateRecursively (:596, unconditional) -> _deactivate assert :625 fails. Repro: pump threw 'Failed assertion: line 625 ... _lifecycle == _ElementLifecycle.active', and the fail-closed catch (:598-609) unmounted the subtree: disp=1, act=0 — GlobalKey State destroyed on a legitimate reparent. Release consequence traced: :626 recomputes _hadDependenciesWhenDeactivated from already-cleared sets, defeating :670-673's forced rebuild (the behavior reparenting_test.dart:457 pins as load-bearing). Flutter guards this with 'if (element._active)' in _InactiveElements.add; Fleury has no guard.
v1.fix_sketch: In _deactivateChild (or _deactivateRecursively), only run the recursive deactivation when child._lifecycle == active (mirroring Flutter's _InactiveElements.add guard); still null _parent and add to _inactiveElements.

findings.title: ConstrainedBox re-breaks min<=max after reconciliation, producing impossible child constraints that crash layout under Expanded or stretch parents
findings.failure_scenario: Row(children: [Expanded(child: ConstrainedBox(maxWidth: 20, child: Text(...)))]) on an 80-col surface: Expanded delivers tight main-axis constraints (minCols == maxCols == 80); _RenderConstrainedBox computes maxC=20, fixes minC down to 20, then the parent-min re-clamp raises minC back to 80 -> CellConstraints(minCols: 80, maxCols: 20). In dev (asserts on) this throws AssertionError 'maxCols must be >= minCols when bounded' (layout.dart:20); in release no size can satisfy min>max so the child's layout() throws FleuryError. Either way the subtree dies and the nearest ErrorBoundary renders a red error panel instead of the content. Same failure for ConstrainedBox(maxHeight: N) under CrossAxisAlignment.stretch. Both variants reproduced empirically in a test harness at HEAD; Flutter's enforce semantics (parent wins for max too) renders this fine.
findings.evidence: basic.dart:947 fixes minC (`if (maxC != null && minC > maxC) minC = maxC;`) but basic.dart:948 (`if (minC < constraints.minCols) minC = constraints.minCols;`) then raises minC above maxC again (identical pattern for rows at :955-956); the invalid CellConstraints is constructed at :958. render_flex.dart:598-622 shows FlexFit.tight delivers minMain == allocation (tight), and layout.dart:17-26 asserts max >= min while render_object.dart:1089 throws FleuryError when the returned size fails isSatisfiedBy — with min>max every size fails. Verified: dart test probe shows AssertionError at basic.dart:958 via RenderFlexible.performLayout for the Expanded case and via RenderFlex pass-1 for the stretch case, while RenderSizedBox (render_objects.dart:627-628) handles the same conflict correctly with constrainWidth.

v1.why: Traced: RenderFlex._childConstraintsForFlexible (render_flex.dart:603-607) gives Expanded tight minCols=maxCols=80; RenderFlexible passes them through (render_flex.dart:111-113). _RenderConstrainedBox (basic.dart:942-948): maxC stays 20 (only clamped down, never raised to parent min), line 947 drops minC to 20, line 948 raises it back to 80 -> CellConstraints(minCols:80, maxCols:20) at :958 trips assert layout.dart:19-22 in dev; in release the child can satisfy no size so layout() throws FleuryError (render_object.dart:1089-1105). Contradicts the code's own comment 'Parent bounds always win' (basic.dart:938-939). Rows/stretch variant identical (:955-956, render_flex.dart:584-586).
v1.fix_sketch: After the parent-min re-clamp, re-raise the max to the min (Flutter enforce semantics): in _RenderConstrainedBox.performLayout add `if (maxC != null && maxC < minC) maxC = minC;` after basic.dart:948 and the same for rows after :956 — parent's tight bound wins over the widget's max.

v2.why: Reproduced at HEAD: both variants throw the exact predicted assert (layout.dart:19-26) from CellConstraints built at basic.dart:958, via RenderFlex.performLayout -> RenderFlexible (Expanded, tight min=max per render_flex.dart:603-607) and via stretch pass-1 (render_flex.dart:277-278). basic.dart:947 drops minC to maxC then :948 re-raises it past maxC (rows: :955-956). Release path: min>max satisfies no size -> FleuryError (render_object.dart:1089). No guard exists; behavior contradicts ConstrainedBox's own doc "the parent's bounds win" (basic.dart:868-871); existing tests cover only loose parents. ErrorBoundary containment (framework.dart:1196-1201) makes it a red panel, not a crash -> P1.
v2.fix_sketch: After the parent-min re-clamps, re-raise max to min (Flutter enforce semantics): add `if (maxC != null && maxC < minC) maxC = minC;` after basic.dart:948 and the rows analogue after :956, and pin with a test for Expanded+maxWidth and stretch+maxHeight.

findings.title: ExcludeFocus does not release focus already held inside the excluded subtree — typed input keeps flowing into the hidden pane
findings.failure_scenario: A TextInput inside ExcludeFocus holds focus; the app flips excluding:true (the widget's own documented IndexedStack tab-switch use). The hidden field STAYS the focused node and keeps receiving all keys and typed text: verified at HEAD with a test — after flipping excluding:true, hiddenNode.hasFocus stayed true and typing 'zz' landed in the hidden pane's controller while the visible pane got nothing. User keystrokes silently mutate invisible state (data misdirection); nothing on screen reacts. Programmatic requestFocus INTO an excluded subtree also succeeds for the same reason.
findings.evidence: Exclusion is only consulted by the traversal/click gates: FocusManager.isClickable (focus.dart ~437) walks _ExcludeFocusMarkerElement ancestors, and isTraversable/_traversalOrder use it — but requestFocus (focus.dart ~527) gates only on canRequestFocus + _acceptsInput (~474), which checks _inputExcludedSubtrees (error containment) and never _activeExcludeFocusMarkers; activeChain (~784) and InputDispatcher._deliverText (input_dispatcher.dart ~285) likewise never consult exclude markers. _registerExcludeFocus (focus.dart ~374) only adds the marker to a set and notifies — it never unfocuses a focused descendant (Flutter's ExcludeFocus does). The class doc (~1490) promises the keyboard can't 'wander into the hidden ones', which the focused-at-exclusion-time path violates.

v1.why: _registerExcludeFocus (focus.dart:374-377) only adds to _activeExcludeFocusMarkers; sole consumer is isClickable (437-447). requestFocus (527-537) gates on canRequestFocus+_acceptsInput (474-478, checks _inputExcludedSubtrees not exclude markers) and activeChain (784-804) likewise, so a focused descendant stays _focusedNode and _deliverText (input_dispatcher.dart:285-294) keeps feeding its claimant. Only focus-clearing paths are lines 199/243. Violates class doc 1492-1493; tests cover only Tab-skip/autofocus-deny (autofocus gated separately at _attach:1182).
v1.fix_sketch: In _registerExcludeFocus (and marker update-to-register), drop focus if _focusedNode sits under an active excluding marker (walk like isClickable); also add the exclude-marker ancestor walk to FocusManager.requestFocus so programmatic focus into an excluded subtree is denied, matching Flutter's ExcludeFocus.

v2.why: Exclusion set's only consumers are isClickable (focus.dart:437-447) and dispose (903) — grep-verified. Flip path (_ExcludeFocusMarkerElement.update 1553-1569 → _registerExcludeFocus 374-377) never releases focus; subtree reconciles in place so _unregister's clear (518-521) can't run. Retained node flows through activeChain (784-804; gates only _acceptsInput 474-478) into _deliverText (input_dispatcher.dart:285-294) and _dispatchPlain — hidden pane receives text and keys. requestFocus (527-537) also skips exclude markers. Decisive: navigator, the sole internal consumer, must self-guard with requestFocus(null) before covering routes (navigator.dart:520-522); the doc-advertised IndexedStack pattern (focus.dart:1492-1493) has no guard, no sanitizing listener exists, and no test pins retention as intended.
v2.fix_sketch: When exclusion activates (marker mount/update-flip and _registerExcludeFocus), walk up from _focusedNode._element with the isClickable ancestor walk and requestFocus(null) on a hit; add the same exclude-marker walk to FocusManager.requestFocus so programmatic focus into an excluded subtree is denied, matching the click/Tab/autofocus gates and Flutter's ExcludeFocus.

findings.title: requestFocus on a FocusNode whose Focus widget was unmounted focuses a dead node: every keypress throws StateError and the Ctrl+C exit guard is bypassed
findings.failure_scenario: App keeps a caller-provided FocusNode (e.g. a search field's node in a parent State); the TextInput using it is conditionally removed; a handler later calls node.requestFocus() while it is hidden (focus-the-field shortcut that doesn't check visibility). requestFocus SUCCEEDS (verified: focus=true while attachedNodes does not contain the node). activeChain() = [dead node] whose onKey is the disposed _TextInputState._handleKey; the next keypress (and typed text, via the synthesized-KeyEvent fallback) throws 'Bad state: State.widget accessed before initState or after dispose' — verified at HEAD. In production every keystroke lands in run_app's per-event catch (error banner), tree KeyBindings/Tab traversal are unreachable (chain walk stops at the dead element), and because the Ctrl+C exit check (run_app.dart:630) sits AFTER dispatcher.dispatch (run_app.dart:624) inside the same try, the throw skips it — Ctrl+C can no longer exit. Keyboard is bricked until a mouse click moves focus or the process is killed externally.
findings.evidence: FocusManager._unregister (focus.dart ~510-523) clears node._manager/_element only on the manager-disposed path; normal unregistration (from _FocusState._detach on deactivate/dispose, ~1188) leaves node._manager and node._element pointing at the live manager and the now-defunct element, and _FocusState.dispose never clears node.onKey on a caller-provided node. requestFocus (~527) validates liveness via _acceptsInput (~474) which only requires node._element != null — true for a defunct element — so _focusedNode is set to a node absent from _attachedNodes. StatefulElement.unmount nulls _state._widget (framework.dart ~1140), so the retained onKey handler's first `widget.enabled` read throws (framework.dart 272-280). Reproduced end-to-end with FleuryTester: both sendKey and type threw the StateError.

v1.why: _unregister (focus.dart:510-523) clears node._manager/_element only when the manager is disposed; _FocusState._detach (1188-1193) leaves them stale and onKey (=_TextInputState._handleKey, set 1106/1137, text_input.dart:1515) uncleared. node.requestFocus (236-239) then passes _acceptsInput (474-478: stale _element != null) and focuses the dead node. Next KeyEvent: activeChain=[dead node] -> _handleKey reads widget.enabled (text_input.dart:1231) -> StateError (framework.dart:1140 nulls _widget, getter throws 272-280). Throw at run_app.dart:624 jumps to catch (654), skipping the Ctrl+C exit check (630-637) in the same try.
v1.fix_sketch: Clear node._element (and _manager) on the normal _unregister path, not just the manager-disposed branch; _acceptsInput then denies dead nodes and FocusNode.requestFocus no-ops per its own doc ('no-op when not attached'). The sanctioned remount-reuse pattern (focus.dart:490-501) still works because _register re-sets both.

v2.why: Independently traced at HEAD: focus.dart:510-523 clears node._manager/_element only on the disposed branch; _detach (1188-1193) plus provided-node dispose leave _element and onKey (_handleKey, text_input.dart:1515) stale. requestFocus (236-239, 527-537) passes _acceptsInput (474-478); activeChain (784-788) yields the dead node; dispatcher calls onKey unguarded (input_dispatcher.dart:391-392) so widget.enabled (text_input.dart:1231) throws (framework.dart unmount nulls _widget). run_app.dart:624 dispatch precedes the Ctrl+C check (630-637) in one try; catch (654) skips it. cfmakeraw raw mode (posix_driver.dart:79-84, 325) makes Ctrl+C in-band — SIGINT watcher (311-314) only rescues external kill. focus_test.dart:115-157 pins only the disposed-manager variant. Mouse click-to-focus and pre-dispatch debug hotkeys remain as recovery, so P1 not P0.
v2.fix_sketch: In _unregister's normal path, clear node._element, node._manager, and node._enclosingScope (as the disposed branch does); _acceptsInput then fails and FocusNode.requestFocus no-ops per its documented contract. The sanctioned remount-reuse pattern is unaffected since _register (focus.dart:502-507) re-sets both. Optionally also skip chain nodes whose element is defunct in InputDispatcher as defense-in-depth.

findings.title: Paint-only updates that shrink/move content leave stale ghost cells on screen: diffBounds excludes vacated cells so the bounded diff never erases them
findings.failure_scenario: App has `RepaintBoundary(child: IndexedStack(index: 0, children: [wideChild, narrowChild]))` (or any ListView item — rows are auto-wrapped in RepaintBoundary by default). Frame N shows the wide child, e.g. 'LONG-LONG-TEXT'. setState switches `index` to 1 — `RenderIndexedStack.index` calls `markNeedsPaintOnly()`, which records only a visual change, never `requiresFullDiff`. The boundary repaints its cache with just 'X'; the blit into the frame buffer records damage only over the tight NEW content box (1x1). Confirmed by repro at HEAD: frame 2 has `requiresFullDiff: false`, `diffBounds: CellRect((0,0), 1x1)`, 13 cells differ between prev/next OUTSIDE diffBounds, and the production presenter emits only `ESC[HX` (ground-truth unbounded diff emits `ESC[HX` + 13 spaces). The terminal permanently shows 'XONG-LONG-TEXT' until some unrelated change happens to smear damage over the region. Same mechanism hits Overlay's `_Visibility.visible` flips (overlay.dart:558), `RenderNavigatorStack.firstPainted` (render_navigator.dart:42, a settling opaque route hides content-sized routes below), `RenderText.textAlign`/`overflow` (render_objects.dart:171/182 — 'Hi' aligned left->right leaves a duplicate 'Hi' at the old columns), `RenderBorder.border`, and TextArea caret cells past end-of-text. The serve/DOM row-oriented presenter has the same hole for fully-vacated rows (rows never written are absent from paintDamageRows).
findings.evidence: `markNeedsPaintOnly` (render_object.dart:969-973) records only `recordVisualChange()`, not `recordLayoutOrConservativePaint()`, so `TuiFrameDamage.requiresFullDiff` stays false and `diffBounds => paintDamageBounds` (tui_frame_loop.dart:164-165). Damage is recorded only for cells WRITTEN into the back buffer (`next.clear()` runs under `withoutDamageTracking`, tui_frame_loop.dart:80); nothing ever records the PREVIOUS frame's painted extent. On boundary repaint, render_repaint_boundary.dart:295-298 replaces `_cacheBounds` with the new tight box (no union with the old bounds) and line 350 `buffer.copyRectFrom(cacheForCopy, bounds, destOffset)` records damage over the new box only; all clean boundaries blit under `withoutDamageTracking` (line 352-354). `AnsiFramePresenter.presentFrame` passes `dirtyBounds: frame.damage.diffBounds` to `renderDiff` (ansi_frame_presenter.dart:108), whose contract (ansi_renderer.dart:139-141) requires the bounds to 'conservatively contain every cell that can differ' — violated here. End-to-end repro run at HEAD (dart test, RenderRepaintBoundary + RenderIndexedStack + TuiFrameLoop + AnsiRenderer) reproduced the stale bytes exactly.

v1.why: Reproduced at HEAD with a throwaway test. Chain: RenderIndexedStack.index= (render_stack.dart:224-228) -> markNeedsPaintOnly (render_object.dart:969-973, records only recordVisualChange); element update adds no invalidation (framework.dart:1657-1665); boundary replaces _cacheBounds with the tight new box, no union with old extent (render_repaint_boundary.dart:295-298), tracked blit covers new box only (line 350); diffBounds=paintDamageBounds (tui_frame_loop.dart:164-165) -> renderDiff scans only that rect. Repro: requiresFullDiff=false, diffBounds 1x1, 13 cells differ outside bounds; bytes ESC[HX vs ground-truth ESC[HX+13 spaces. After commit both buffers agree cells are empty, so the ghost is permanent. ListView rows auto-wrap in RepaintBoundary (list_view.dart:388,607).
v1.fix_sketch: In RenderRepaintBoundary.paint, when repainting, record damage over the UNION of the previous _cacheBounds and the new bounds (e.g. buffer.recordDamage over the old rect, or blit-with-damage over old-union-new); alternatively make markNeedsPaintOnly call sites that change painted extent (IndexedStack.index, visibility flips, textAlign/overflow) record conservative damage.

findings.title: Startup probe replies leak into app input as a phantom Shift+F3 keypress whenever terminal-reply RTT exceeds ~150 ms (any slow SSH session)
findings.failure_scenario: App started over SSH (TERM=xterm-256color — ssh never forwards TERM_PROGRAM, so the image protocol is env-inconclusive and the kitty-graphics probe runs), terminal-reply RTT > 150 ms. enter() runs the image probe, which times out at 150 ms; the ambiguous-width probe starts immediately and its request() cancels the late-probe drain and clears the probe buffer. The image probe's late DA reply then lands inside the width probe's window and satisfies its DA sentinel (_looksComplete), so the width probe returns 'complete' and _replayPostProbeInput clears all diversion state. The width probe's own CPR reply (ESC[1;2R) arrives one RTT later with no probe or drain active, is fed to InputParser, and CSI final 'R' decodes as KeyCode.f3 with modifier param 2 = shift — the app receives KeyEvent(shift+f3) the user never typed, on every startup. Reproduced deterministically with a scripted fake terminal: RTT 170/260/350/500/700 ms all deliver KeyEvent(shift+f3) to driver.events; RTT ≤ 120 ms is clean. The single-probe path (TERM=xterm-kitty, only the width probe runs) leaks the same phantom key for any RTT > ~400 ms because the late-drain grace is fixed at 250 ms. In an app that binds F3 (file managers, MC-style UIs) this triggers a real action at launch.
findings.evidence: _DriverProbeTransport.request polls until _looksComplete(_driver._probeBuffer) (posix_driver.dart:1057-1062), and _looksComplete/_daReplyEnd (posix_driver.dart:1070, 1079-1091) accept ANY Device-Attributes reply as the stop sentinel — including the PREVIOUS probe's late reply; request() also begins with _cancelLateProbeDrain() (posix_driver.dart:1051), discarding the protection armed for probe 1. _replayPostProbeInput (posix_driver.dart:472-493) then finds that stale DA, treats the exchange as settled, and arms no drain, so the second probe's straggling CPR flows through the normal listener into _parser.feed (posix_driver.dart:368). input_parser.dart:493-507 maps CSI final 0x52 ('R') to KeyCode.f3 and _decodeModifiers(2) = shift. Repro script: scratchpad parser_probe/probe_race.dart (fake tty stdin/stdout, per-query RTT delay) — output 'events seen by the app: [KeyEvent(shift+f3)]' at HEAD. The PR #95-era drain hardening (276a321, 477d6a4) covers only a single probe within the 250 ms grace.

v1.why: Traced full chain at HEAD: enter() -> _maybeProbeImageProtocol (posix_driver.dart:390,412; runs for ssh-default env per capabilities.dart:348-365), 150ms timeout (terminal_probe.dart:164) -> _replayPostProbeInput arms 250ms drain (posix_driver.dart:491-492) -> width probe request() cancels drain + clears buffer (1051-1053) -> image probe's late DA satisfies _daReplyEnd (1070,1079-1091, accepts ANY ESC[..c) -> _replayPostProbeInput finds stale DA, arms no drain (472-483) -> width probe's CPR ESC[1;2R leaks to _parser.feed (368) -> final 0x52 = KeyCode.f3 (input_parser.dart:502), _decodeModifiers(2)={shift} (595-598). No guard interrupts this.
v1.fix_sketch: Make the probe-completion sentinel unambiguous per exchange: tag each DA request with a unique parameter the reply echoes, or count outstanding DA replies so probe 2 waits for the Nth DA (and _replayPostProbeInput likewise consumes through the LAST expected DA, arming the drain when fewer have landed). Minimal alternative: don't _cancelLateProbeDrain in request(); instead have request() first absorb the pending drain (wait for probe 1's DA or grace) before writing probe 2's query.

v2.why: Traced and reproduced at HEAD. Chain: image probe runs under ssh-default env (capabilities.dart:348-366; posix_driver.dart:418-421), times out at 150ms, arms drain (posix_driver.dart:491-492); width probe request() cancels it and clears the buffer (1051-1053); probe 1's late DA satisfies _daReplyEnd — accepts ANY ESC[..c (1070,1079-1091) — so _replayPostProbeInput (473-484) arms no drain; the width probe's CPR then hits _parser.feed (368) → ESC[1;2R = f3+shift (input_parser.dart:502,590-598); runApp's pre-enter subscription guarantees delivery (run_app.dart:870-885). Repro test (fake TTY, event-driven timing) observed exactly one KeyEvent(f3,{shift}); deleted after. No guard, no fix in HEAD commits (8e94c26, 477d6a4 are single-probe only).
v2.fix_sketch: Make the DA stop-sentinel exchange-specific: count DA queries written vs DA replies seen (driver-level counter), so probe 2's _looksComplete and _replayPostProbeInput require the LAST owed DA and arm the drain when fewer have landed. Alternative: request() absorbs the prior exchange first (waits for its DA or the remaining grace) instead of _cancelLateProbeDrain().

findings.title: Warm-standby app self-destructs 10s after spawn: enter()'s INIT timeout kills an idle pre-spawned session, silently defeating the cold-start optimization
findings.failure_scenario: Start `fleury serve --spawn dart run my_app.dart`. prepareWarm() (bin/fleury.dart:1005/1031) spawns the initial standby; its app process connects to the session socket and calls runApp -> RemoteTerminalDriver.enter() (run_app.dart:904), which blocks in `_handshake.future.timeout(initTimeout)` awaiting the browser's INIT. Serve synthesizes NO INIT for an unattached standby (the only InitFrame producer is the shell path, bin/fleury.dart:334); INIT only arrives when a browser attaches and _pumpBytes forwards it. Open the browser ~12s later (or any time >10s after the standby was prepared). The standby's enter() already hit its 10s deadline: it cancelled _frameSub, called `_transport.close()`, and threw, so the subprocess exited; bringUp's `_app!.process.exitCode.then((_) => shutdown())` (bin/fleury.dart:1258) fired and prepareWarm's done-handler set `warm=null`. The browser connect finds `warm==null` and falls back to a COLD `_SpawnSession.start` (bin/fleury.dart:1131), paying the full multi-second `dart run` VM+JIT cold-compile latency the warm standby existed to eliminate. Because every connect prepares a fresh standby with the same ~10s TTL, any two connects spaced >10s apart both pay cold-start cost. Additionally, a browser connecting right at the ~10s boundary can pass the `claimed.isReady` check (bin/fleury.dart:1118) yet have the standby's enter() time out during attach(), forwarding INIT into a closing transport -> an immediately-dead session (blank flash + forced reconnect).
findings.evidence: remote_driver.dart:171 bounds the INIT wait to `initTimeout` (=10s, line 186) and on TimeoutException cancels the frame subscription and calls `_transport.close()` (lines 172-180), permanently killing the app's connection. The warm-standby design at bin/fleury.dart:1227-1231 explicitly assumes the opposite: the app "blocks awaiting INIT ... before any connection, and a later attach pairs instantly" — i.e. it expects an unbounded wait until attach. The initTimeout rationale comment (remote_driver.dart:164-169) assumes "the bridge/serve client sends INIT immediately on accept," which holds for the COLD path (_SpawnSession.start buffers the already-connected browser's INIT via BufferedBrowserInput before spawning, bin/fleury.dart:1301-1310) but is false for the warm path, where bringUp (bin/fleury.dart:1239) spawns the app BEFORE any browser exists. spawnFleuryApp returns as soon as the app connects (which resolves `warm`/isReady=true), and the app then starts its 10s enter() clock with no INIT source — so the standby's effective TTL is ~10s. The two independently-hardened features (initTimeout leak-guard vs. warm pre-spawn) contradict each other; no test overrides initTimeout for the warm-idle case (only remote_driver_test.dart:383 shortens it).

v1.why: Traced full chain: prepareWarm (bin/fleury.dart:1031) -> bringUp (:1232) spawns via spawnFleuryApp (spawn.dart:65) which sends nothing; app resolves FLEURY_HANDLE -> RemoteTerminalDriver (run_app.dart:1482-1487), enter() (run_app.dart:904) waits on _handshake with 10s initTimeout (remote_driver.dart:171,186); handshake completes only on InitFrame (:480), whose sole serve-path source is the browser pumped by attach (bin/fleury.dart:1265-1283). No override exists (only test remote_driver_test.dart:383). Timeout closes transport + throws -> app exits -> exitCode handler (:1258) -> warm=null (:1017-1022) -> next connect >10s later goes cold (:1131). Guard commit 45ad9c0 (07-10) postdates warm feature b39f8d7 (06-14). P1: defeats the optimization on the common path, spams errors every run, boundary race yields dead sessions.
v1.fix_sketch: The F9 leak-guard's rationale (silent peer on the shell/bridge accept path) does not apply to serve-supervised spawns: serve already kills orphans (abort/dispose, admission cap). Root-cause fix: make the wait mode explicit — bringUp's spawnEnv sets e.g. FLEURY_REMOTE_WAIT=supervised, and RemoteTerminalDriver.enter() waits unbounded (or arms the timeout only after first transport activity) when set; keep the 10s fuse for unsupervised connects. Add a serve_spawn test with a shortened initTimeout proving a standby older than initTimeout still pairs. Alternatively, serve could proactively recycle standbys before the fuse, but that treats the symptom.

v2.why: Independently traced, refutation failed. bringUp (bin/fleury.dart:1239)→spawnFleuryApp (spawn.dart:65-232) never writes to the socket; sole spawn-path INIT source is a browser pumped by attach (:1265-1283; :334 is shell-only). App: FLEURY_HANDLE→RemoteTerminalDriver (runtime/run_app.dart:1482-1487), enter() awaited (:904), handshake completes only on InitFrame (remote_driver.dart:480), bounded by static 10s initTimeout (:171,:186) — no override (only remote_driver_test.dart:382-384). Timeout closes transport+throws (:172-179)→app exits→shutdown (:1258)→warm=null, no re-prepare (:1017-1022)→cold spawn (:1131). Fuse 45ad9c0 (07-10) postdates warm b39f8d7 (06-14); design comment :1227-1231 contradicts it at HEAD. serve_spawn_test.dart:143 pairs <8s, so CI can't catch it. Any connect >10s after the previous goes cold: common path.
v2.fix_sketch: Make the wait mode explicit: bringUp/start set e.g. FLEURY_REMOTE_WAIT=supervised in spawnEnv, and RemoteTerminalDriver.enter() waits unbounded under it (serve already reaps orphans via dispose/abort + admission cap), keeping the 10s fuse for unsupervised connects. Add a serve_spawn test with shortened initTimeout proving a standby older than the fuse still pairs.

findings.title: Retained-leaf semantic flush ships a stale tree (release) or throws the divergence StateError (debug) when a non-SemanticsElement contributor changes in the same frame as a Text/leaf update
findings.failure_scenario: Serve/web/embed session (FrameSemanticsPipeline is the shared engine). App shows a DataTable plus a status Text ('selected: 0'). User presses arrow-down: DataTable's selectedRow/selection state changes (its _DataTableElement is a SemanticContributor but never records into SemanticDirtyTracker) and the status Text updates to 'selected: 2' (Text wraps in Semantics(includeChildren:false), so its element update records only recordLeafDirty). The flush's canApplyRetainedLeafUpdates sees requiresFullRebuild=false + leafUpdates nonempty and patches ONLY the Text node via retainedTree.replaceNodes — the DataTable's semantic node (value/selectedKey, selectionRange, sort/filter state, synthesized row children) stays stale. REPRODUCED at HEAD with first-party widgets: takeDirtySnapshot returned requiresFullRebuild:false, leafUpdates=[the Text node], and debugSemanticTreeDivergence(fromElement(root), replaced) reported divergence at the table node. In JIT/dev (dart run, asserts on) the pipeline's own assert throws StateError('Retained semantic leaf replacement diverged...') from flushNow and _runScheduledFlush rethrows it into the scheduler task — repeating on every such interaction; in AOT release the wire silently ships the stale tree, so MCP agents and screen readers read the wrong selection/filter/rows until a later flush with an empty leaf-update set forces a full walk. Same trigger exists for _FleuryAppSemanticsElement (lastCommandId/lastCommandStatus/status count) and _CommandScopeSemanticsElement: run any command whose visible effect is a Text change.
findings.evidence: frame_semantics_pipeline.dart:273-285 — canApplyRetainedLeafUpdates requires only !requiresFullRebuild && leafUpdates.isNotEmpty (plus containment); it does NOT consult _semanticDirty, the flag run_app.dart:625 sets on every dispatched input precisely because 'some state a handler mutates is only visible on the next tree walk' (comment at frame_semantics_pipeline.dart:113-117); _semanticDirty only gates the retain-output fast path (line 255-260) and is then cleared at line 351 without the walk having happened. The tracker only sees SemanticsElement/_ExcludeSemanticsElement lifecycle events (semantics.dart:1241-1275, 1453-1485) — grep shows no recordStructureDirty/recordLeafDirty in _DataTableElement (packages/fleury_widgets/lib/src/data_table.dart:1176-1268, buildSemanticNode reads renderObject.visibleFirst/selectedRow/sort state live), _FleuryAppSemanticsElement (packages/fleury/lib/src/app/app.dart:546-560, update() just super.update+rebuild), or _CommandScopeSemanticsElement (packages/fleury/lib/src/app/commands.dart:447-458). Text is Semantics(includeChildren:false) (packages/fleury/lib/src/widgets/basic.dart:128-132) so its updates take the leaf path (semantics.dart:1247-1256). The debug assert that catches the divergence and throws is frame_semantics_pipeline.dart:291-309; the pipeline is wired for every serve session at run_app.dart:1160-1170.

v1.why: Traced+reproduced. Chain: run_app.dart:625 input dispatch mutates contributor state + Text, then markSemanticsDirty(); Text=Semantics(includeChildren:false) (basic.dart:~127) → recordLeafDirty (semantics.dart:1254); exhaustive grep shows only SemanticsElement/_ExcludeSemanticsElement lifecycle + error_boundary.dart:71 ever record — _DataTableElement (fleury_widgets data_table.dart:1176-1268), _FleuryAppSemanticsElement, _CommandScopeSemanticsElement read live state, record nothing. canApplyRetainedLeafUpdates (frame_semantics_pipeline.dart:273-281) ignores _semanticDirty → replaceNodes(:284) skips the walk. My repro test: snapshot={1 leaf, no fullRebuild}, containment holds, divergence at contributor node. Correction: dart run defaults asserts OFF (verified) and no --enable-asserts anywhere, so real serve sessions silently ship the stale tree; the StateError fires only under dart test.
v1.fix_sketch: Preserve the leaf-path optimization by making contributor state changes visible to the tracker: record recordStructureDirty() in the update()/state-change paths of _DataTableElement, _FleuryAppSemanticsElement, _CommandScopeSemanticsElement (and any SemanticContributor whose buildSemanticNode reads live state), or document/enforce that contract via a SemanticContributor hook. Cheaper engine-level alternative: add `&& !_semanticDirty` to frame_semantics_pipeline.dart:273 so input-triggered flushes always take the full walk (matches the line 114-117 contract, but disables the leaf path for most real updates). Also consider running the divergence oracle in serve dev sessions since asserts are off under dart run.

findings.title: ProcessTaskController.dispose() never kills the running child process — verified live child after dispose
findings.failure_scenario: A widget State owns a ProcessTaskController and starts a long-lived process (`npm run watch`, `tail -f`, `sleep 30`). The screen is popped and State.dispose() calls controller.dispose(). The controller marks the run canceled (TaskController.dispose records TaskEventKind.canceled), but no signal is ever sent: the child keeps running indefinitely, and the still-subscribed stdout/stderr decoders keep draining its output (CPU/RAM burn for chatty children like `yes`). Verified by probe: after controller.dispose(), `kill -0 <pid>` shows the `sleep 30` child still alive; nothing in the API can kill it anymore (cancel()/reset() throw or no-op after dispose).
findings.evidence: ProcessTaskController overrides only cancel() (process_task.dart:104-110) and reset() (:113-120), both of which kill _process — but there is no dispose() override, and the base TaskController.dispose (task.dart:337-352) sets _disposed/_status directly without calling the virtual cancel(), so the kill in the subclass is bypassed. Worse, dispose() bumps _runId (task.dart:341) before setting _cancelRequested, so TaskContext.isCancellationRequested (task.dart:206-207, gated on _isCurrent(_runId)) returns false forever for the in-flight run — the runner cannot even observe the cancellation cooperatively. Probe on this worktree confirmed pid alive after dispose.

v1.why: ProcessTaskController defines no dispose() override (process_task.dart:65-214; only cancel :104-110 and reset :112-120 kill). Base TaskController.dispose (task.dart:337-352) mutates state directly and never calls the virtual cancel(), so no signal is sent. Afterward the kill is unreachable: cancel() gates on isRunning (process_task.dart:106) which dispose made false (task.dart:345), and reset() throws via _checkNotDisposed (task.dart:321,354). Cooperative cancel also dead: dispose bumps _runId (:341) before _cancelRequested (:342), and isCancellationRequested requires _isCurrent (:206-207). Chain: State.dispose -> controller.dispose -> child never killed. Minor overstatement: the still-live handle remains reachable via the public process getter (:82).
v1.fix_sketch: Override dispose() in ProcessTaskController: if isRunning, _process?.kill(_cancelSignal); _process = null; then super.dispose(). Optionally have base dispose route through cancel() when running.

v2.why: Traced chain: State.dispose → controller.dispose = inherited TaskController.dispose (task.dart:337-352), which never calls the virtual cancel(), so ProcessTaskController's kill (process_task.dart:107) is bypassed. Afterward cancel() no-ops (isRunning false, process_task.dart:106 vs task.dart:345), reset() throws (task.dart:321), and cooperative cancel is dead (_runId bumped at task.dart:341 makes TaskContext stale; task.dart:206-207) — the orphaned run awaits exitCode forever (process_task.dart:173). No caller cancels first (ProcessPanel is app-owned-controller), no test pins dispose-while-running, and decision-log.md (2026-06-02) documents dispose-cancels-the-active-run as the contract. Minor overstatement: `process` getter (:82) still exposes the live handle. Leak, not crash → P1.
v2.fix_sketch: Override dispose() in ProcessTaskController: if (isRunning) _process?.kill(_cancelSignal); then null _process/_command and call super.dispose() (isRunning must be read before super flips status). Alternatively have base TaskController.dispose route through cancel() when running so all subclasses inherit the fix.

findings.title: startProcess with any non-normal ProcessStartMode always fails the task and permanently orphans the just-spawned child
findings.failure_scenario: Developer runs an interactive child the way the API invites: `controller.startProcess(cmd, handoffTerminal: true, mode: ProcessStartMode.inheritStdio)` (mode is a first-class parameter here and on ProcessCommandRunner). The child spawns, then _runProcess immediately throws `Bad state: stdio is not connected` when touching process.stdout; the task flips to failed. Because the throw happens before the try/finally at :172-183, _process is never cleared but the task is no longer running, so cancel()/reset() refuse to kill — the child is orphaned forever. With handoffTerminal: true this is worse: the handoff's finally re-enters the TUI and repaints while the interactive child still owns the terminal, so both write to the tty (screen corruption + stdin race). Verified by probe: result=failed 'Bad state: stdio is not connected', `sleep 5` child still alive afterwards with no way to kill it via the controller.
findings.evidence: _runProcess unconditionally pipes process.stdout/process.stderr (process_task.dart:161-170) regardless of the `mode` argument accepted at :90 and forwarded from ProcessCommandRunner (:33/:56 of process_command_runner.dart); dart:io throws StateError on the stdio getters for inheritStdio/detached modes. The stream setup is outside the try block starting at :172, so the `finally` that clears _process (:181-183) never runs, and TaskController._run records the error as a failed result (task.dart:394-406), leaving isRunning false so cancel() (:105 `if (isRunning)`) never kills.
also: fw-shell: ProcessPanel/ProcessTaskController: task stays 'running' indefinitely after the child exits when a backgrounded grandchild holds the stdio pipes

v1.why: _runProcess pipes process.stdout/stderr unconditionally (process_task.dart:161-170) regardless of mode (:90, forwarded from ProcessCommandRunner.start :67); dart:io stdio getters throw StateError for inheritStdio/detached. The throw is outside the try at :172, so the finally clearing _process (:181-183) never runs; the error reaches TaskController._run's catch (task.dart:394-406), isRunning goes false, and cancel/reset's isRunning guards (process_task.dart:106,114) skip the kill — spawned child orphaned. With handoffTerminal, the handoff wrapper (terminal_driver.dart:164-173) resumes the TUI after the throw while the inheritStdio child owns the tty. external_editor.dart:202,270 shows inheritStdio+handoff is the intended interactive pattern; every non-normal mode of this advertised param fails 100%.
v1.fix_sketch: Only pipe stdio for modes that connect it (normal/detachedWithStdio) and skip exitCode for detached, or reject unsupported modes with ArgumentError before spawning; move spawn+pipe setup inside try/finally so _process is killed/cleared on failure.

v2.why: Traced full chain; probe-verified SDK semantics: `.stdout` throws 'Bad state: stdio is not connected' for inheritStdio/detached, `.exitCode` throws for detachedWithStdio — every non-normal mode fails. process_task.dart:161-170 pipes unconditionally before the try at :172, so the finally (:181-183) never clears `_process`; error lands in task.dart:394-406 → failed → isRunning false (task.dart:270) → cancel/reset kill guards (process_task.dart:106,114) skip — child leaks (only manual `controller.process?.kill()` via the :82 getter remains, and reset() discards it). posix_driver.dart:755-781 re-enters raw mode and repaints while the child holds the tty; :697 documents inheritStdio-in-handoff as the supported pattern. No validation, no test covers mode, no fix at HEAD.
v2.fix_sketch: In _runProcess, wire stdout/stderr only for modes that connect stdio (normal, detachedWithStdio) and reject detached modes with ArgumentError up front (a task needs an exit code); move everything after Process.start inside the try/finally, and make cancel/reset kill whenever _process is non-null (kill the child on setup failure too).

findings.title: Restarting a running task makes cancellation unobservable to the superseded run: cooperative runners become CPU zombies, and a restart during the spawn window leaks the previous process unkillable
findings.failure_scenario: Two verified manifestations. (a) Cooperative: a runner loops with TaskYieldCheckpoint.tick()/checkCancellation (the documented pattern for CPU-heavy indexing/filtering). Calling controller.start(newRunner) while it runs (restart: true default, also DebouncedTaskController.runNow) is supposed to cancel it — but the old runner never observes cancellation and runs to natural completion alongside the new one (probe: oldSawCancel=false, all 50 ticks executed). Repeated restarts stack N concurrent loops; an indefinite poll-until-canceled runner leaks forever. (b) Process: two startProcess calls in quick succession (double-tap on a Run command, or two calls in one turn — the first Process.start is always still pending) leave the first child permanently alive: probe showed `sleep 1237` and `sleep 1238` both running, and after cancel() only 1238 died — 1237 survives with no controller path to kill it.
findings.evidence: TaskContext.isCancellationRequested requires `_controller._isCurrent(_runId) && _controller._cancelRequested` (task.dart:206-207). start() while running calls cancel() then synchronously does `++_runId; _cancelRequested = false` (task.dart:287-291) with no await in between, so the superseded runner can never poll the flag while it is set, and afterwards _isCurrent(oldRunId) is false forever. For (b): ProcessTaskController.cancel kills via `_process?.kill` (process_task.dart:107) but _process is still null during `await Process.start` (:127-136); the stale run's own post-spawn check `context.isCancellationRequested` (:137) is dead because its runId is stale, so the first child is never killed, and its later `_process = process` assignment (:136) can even clobber the new run's handle so cancel() targets the wrong process.

v1.why: start() while running executes cancel(); ++_runId; _cancelRequested=false synchronously (task.dart:287-291) — no await, so on a single-threaded event loop the superseded runner can never poll while the flag is set, and afterwards _isCurrent(oldRunId) is false so isCancellationRequested (:206-207), checkCancellation (:209) and TaskYieldCheckpoint.tick (:176) are permanently false: the old runner runs to natural completion (forever, if poll-until-canceled). Same for DebouncedTaskController.runNow (:560-570); restart:true is the default. Existing test (task_test.dart:190-227) pins only result/output isolation, not observation. Process variant: _process is null during await Process.start (process_task.dart:127-135) so cancel's kill (:107) no-ops, the stale :137 check uses a stale runId, and stale _process=process (:136) can clobber the new run's handle. Mitigation note: schedule() with nonzero delay cancels before the delayed start, giving the old runner an observation window — the pure-typeahead path is partly mitigated.
v1.fix_sketch: Make stale runs read as canceled: isCancellationRequested => !_isCurrent(_runId) || _cancelRequested. In ProcessTaskController, keep the spawned process per-run, kill it when the run is stale after spawn, and never assign a stale process to _process.

v2.why: Reproduced both. (a) start()-while-running runs cancel() then synchronously ++_runId; _cancelRequested=false (task.dart:287-291); a suspended runner resumes only afterwards, when isCancellationRequested (task.dart:206-207) is permanently false. Repro: superseded runner ran 5/5 iterations, never observed cancel, its future reported canceled; direct-cancel control stopped at 1/5. (b) Two synchronous startProcess calls (restart:true default, process_task.dart:87): _process is null during await Process.start, so cancel's kill no-ops (:107, :127-136) and the stale :137 check is dead — repro: first child ran to completion. Not intended: :137-141 and TaskYieldPolicy docs (task.dart:127-132) show cancellation should reach in-flight runs. Caller-side mitigations are partial only (ProcessCommandRunner restart:false at process_command_runner.dart:30; nonzero-delay debounce).
v2.fix_sketch: Treat staleness as cancellation: isCancellationRequested => !_controller._isCurrent(_runId) || _controller._cancelRequested. In ProcessTaskController, hold the spawned Process per-run (closure/local), kill it post-spawn when the context reads canceled-or-stale, and only assign _process when the run is still current (mirror the identical-guard already used in the finally at process_task.dart:182).

findings.title: TickerMode(enabled: false) is silently ignored by Animation/AnimationBuilder — hidden subtrees keep animating and rebuilding
findings.failure_scenario: App wraps a hidden pane in TickerMode(enabled: false) exactly as ticker_mode.dart's doc prescribes ('so spinners etc. don't burn CPU in the background'); the pane contains an AnimationBuilder or a raw Animation (e.g. a loop()). Trigger: any retarget or loop. Outcome: the animation runs at full speed — empirically verified: value reached 0.5 at 50ms and settled at 1.0 under TickerMode(false) — so the hidden subtree keeps rebuilding at ~30 Hz (forever, for loop()), while a sibling Spinner in the same subtree correctly freezes. The documented muting lever does nothing for the framework's flagship animation primitive, and animation.dart's header (lines 19-21) explicitly claims 'Animation ... respects AnimationPolicy + TickerMode through the Ticker it owns'.
findings.evidence: Animation.attach (animation.dart:228-239) creates `_ticker = Ticker(_tick, scheduler: _scheduler!)` (line 232) and nothing ever sets `_ticker.muted` or reads TickerMode for it — grep shows the only TickerMode->muted wiring is SingleTickerProviderStateMixin (widgets/tui_binding.dart:210,232) and FrameBuilder (widgets/frame_builder.dart:113-114). TickerMode has zero framework instantiation sites, so it exists purely as this user lever. Ticker.muted's own doc (animation/ticker.dart:68-72) claims it is 'Used by TickerMode to suspend animation in hidden subtrees'. Confirmed by running a probe test at HEAD: Animation.to() inside TickerMode(enabled:false) animated normally.

v1.why: Animation.attach (animation.dart:232) builds a bare Ticker; no code path sets its muted or reads TickerMode — grep shows the only wiring is SingleTickerProviderStateMixin (tui_binding.dart:210,215,232) and FrameBuilder (frame_builder.dart:113-114); Spinner uses FrameBuilder (spinner.dart:75) so it freezes while AnimationBuilder keeps ticking. Chain: AnimationBuilder build -> value getter (animation.dart:187-197) -> attach -> Ticker._handleSchedulerTick (ticker.dart:136, _muted never true) -> _tick -> _notify -> markNeedsBuild each tick. animation.dart:19-21 header falsely claims TickerMode respect; ticker_mode_test.dart never covers Animation.
v1.fix_sketch: Wire TickerMode into Animation: when an element subscribes (value getter / addDependent) resolve TickerMode.enabledOf(context) plus AnimationPolicy and set _ticker.muted, and keep it in sync on dependency change (e.g. AnimationBuilder gains didChangeDependencies that pushes TickerMode.enabledOf into its Animation; the value-getter attach path can do the same for raw Animations, re-resolved per subscribing element).

v2.why: Animation.attach (animation.dart:232) builds a raw Ticker, bypassing TickerProvider.createTicker — the only TickerMode wiring (tui_binding.dart:210,232; frame_builder.dart:113). Exhaustive grep: no `.muted=` reachable from Animation; scheduler fires all callbacks unconditionally (ticker_scheduler.dart:235-242); _muted defaults false (ticker.dart:52,136). Chain: AnimationBuilder build → value getter (animation.dart:187-197) → attach → to()/loop() → _ensureTicking → _tick → _notify → markNeedsBuild ~30 Hz inside TickerMode(false). No guard, no pinning test (ticker_mode_test.dart omits Animation). Docs promise the opposite: animation.dart:19-20 and website guides/animation.md:234-241 ("higher-level tools all do this wiring internally"). AnimationPolicy IS respected (animation.dart:300,485-490), so only the TickerMode claim is false.
v2.fix_sketch: In the value-getter subscription path (and a new AnimationBuilder didChangeDependencies), resolve TickerMode.enabledOf(element) per subscribing element — the element is a BuildContext, so the inherited dependency re-fires on flips — and set _ticker.muted when no enabled dependent remains (also snap/complete loops per policy semantics if desired). Add a ticker_mode_test case pinning Animation/AnimationBuilder freezing under TickerMode(false).

findings.title: FormPanel decimal number fields silently corrupt input: typing "1.5" submits 5 (in-progress "1." wipes the field)
findings.failure_scenario: FormPanel (or FormWizard) with FormFieldSpec.number(allowDecimal: true). User types "1", ".", "5": the moment "." is typed, the field visibly resets to empty and the final value is 5, not 1.5. Editing is equally broken: with "1.5" in the field, backspace over the "5" (leaving "1.") wipes the field; typing "7" yields 7 instead of 1.7. Verified by a widget test at HEAD: controller.value('ratio') == 5 (expected 1.5) and == 7 (expected 1.7). The form silently submits the wrong number. Also reachable via select-all + typing "-" (in-progress token) after a non-null value.
findings.evidence: NumberInput reports null for in-progress tokens ("1.", "-"): number_input.dart:223-227 (_parse returns null for endsWith('.')) and fires onChanged(null) at number_input.dart:252. FormPanel wires that to _controller.setValue(field.id, null) (form.dart:1612). setValue notifies (value transitions 1 -> null, form.dart:712-730), which runs _FormPanelState._onControllerChange -> _syncTextControllers (form.dart:1351-1388). There, _formControlText(field, null) == '' (form.dart:2285-2287), and since controller.text ("1.") != '' the shared TextEditingController is rewritten to '' with caretOffset 0 (form.dart:1382-1385), erasing the user's in-progress text. The next keystroke starts from an empty field. Number fields get no revert protection because the number branch intentionally skips the text listener (form.dart:1378-1380) but _syncTextControllers still rewrites them unconditionally.

v1.why: Traced full chain at HEAD: shared controller (form.dart:1604) → NumberInput._onChanged accepts "1." (_isValid allows trailing dot, number_input.dart:210-216) and fires onChanged(_parse("1."))=null (224-227, 252) → setValue(id, null) (form.dart:1612) → null passes normalize+equality guard, notifyListeners (2262-2269, 716, 729) → _syncTextControllers rewrites controller to _formControlText(field,null)='' with caret 0 (1382-1385, 2286). Number branch skips only the listener (1378-1380), not the rewrite; no focus/editing guard. Next keystroke starts empty → 1.5 becomes 5.
v1.fix_sketch: In _syncTextControllers, don't force-rewrite number fields while the user is editing: for FormFieldType.number skip the rewrite when the field's FocusNode has focus, or when _parseNumberValue(field, controller.text) equals the form value / the text is a valid in-progress token ("1.", "-"). Alternatively have FormPanel keep the last non-null text and only sync on external value changes.

v2.why: Reproduced at HEAD with a throwaway widget test (deleted after): typing 1,.,5 into FormPanel number(allowDecimal:true) yields 5; backspacing 1.5 to "1." then typing 7 yields 7. Chain: NumberInput fires onChanged(null) for "1." (number_input.dart:225,252) → setValue(id,null) (form.dart:1612) → 1→null passes equality guard, synchronous notify (form.dart:716,729; change_notifier.dart:47-51) → _syncTextControllers rewrites shared controller to '' caret 0 (form.dart:1375,1382-1385; _formControlText null→'' at 2286). Number branch skips only the listener (1378-1380); no focus/in-progress guard. Existing test (form_test.dart:1072) types only digits, pins nothing.
v2.fix_sketch: In _syncTextControllers, for FormFieldType.number skip the rewrite when _parseNumberValue(field, controller.text) equals the form value (covers in-progress tokens: null == null), so only genuine external value changes rewrite the text; optionally also skip while the field's FocusNode has focus.

findings.title: DatePicker backward month/year paging lands in the wrong year: PageUp from January jumps 11 months forward; '[' (year back) is a no-op for Feb-Dec
findings.failure_scenario: DatePicker showing January 2026 (value 2026-01-15), user presses PageUp (previous month): selection becomes 2026-12-15 — December of the SAME year, i.e. 11 months forward instead of one back. Pressing '[' (page back one year) on June 2026 emits June 2026 — a no-op; '[' only works when the current month is January. Verified by widget tests at HEAD (expected 2025-12-15, got 2026-12-15; expected 2025-06-15, got 2026-06-15). Forward paging (PageDown, ']') is unaffected. Also hits the '<' header-arrow click, since it calls _shiftMonth(-1) too.
findings.evidence: _shiftMonth (date_picker.dart:212-223) computes targetYear with Dart's truncating division `(v.month - 1 + delta) ~/ 12` (line 216) but targetMonth with floor-modulo `((v.month - 1 + delta) % 12 + 12) % 12 + 1` (line 217). For months-since-January m0 in (-12, 0), `~/` yields 0 (truncation toward zero) while `%` yields the previous-year month, so the year term is off by one whenever the true target is in the previous year: m0 = -1 gives (year+0, December); m0 = -7 gives (year+0, June). Existing test only covers ']' forward paging (date_picker_test.dart:151). Fix is floor division (e.g. `((v.month - 1 + delta) - targetMonth0) / 12` or Dart's `.floorDiv` equivalent).
also: fw-input: DatePicker day-arrow navigation gets stuck on the DST fall-back day and week-moves land one day short (Duration-based date math)

v1.why: Executed the verbatim expressions from date_picker.dart:216-217 in a standalone Dart script: Jan 2026 delta -1 → (2026, 12) not (2025, 12); Jun 2026 delta -12 → (2026, 6) no-op; Jan delta -12 → correct, matching the 'only works from January' claim. Dart ~/ truncates toward zero while % floor-mods, so the year term is off by +1 for months-since-Jan in (-12, 0). Entry points all confirmed: PageUp → _shiftMonth(-1) (line 266), '[' text input → _shiftMonth(-12) (129-130), header '<' click → _shiftMonth(-1) (374). Only ']' forward is tested (date_picker_test.dart:151-162).
v1.fix_sketch: Let DateTime normalize the month instead of hand-rolling: final base = DateTime(v.year, v.month + delta, 1); then use base.year/base.month (Dart normalizes month 0 → Dec of previous year). Or compute true floor division: final m0 = v.month - 1 + delta; targetYear = v.year + ((m0 - (m0 % 12)) ~/ 12). Add backward-paging tests (PageUp from January, '[' from mid-year).

findings.title: FilePicker throws an uncaught PathAccessException (error banner + corrupted state) when entering an unreadable or just-deleted directory
findings.failure_scenario: FilePicker browsing a normal tree; the cursor is on a directory the process cannot read (mode 000 / root-owned / macOS TCC-protected like ~/Library group members, or a directory deleted since listing). User presses Enter/Right or clicks the row: Directory.listSync throws PathAccessException which escapes _onKey uncaught through InputDispatcher.dispatch. Verified by test at HEAD (PathAccessException: Directory listing failed ... errno 13, stack: file_picker.dart:116 -> :188 -> :186 -> :218 -> input_dispatcher.dart). In production runApp's runZonedGuarded backstop reports it as an app fault — the full-screen uncaught-error banner covers the app — and the picker is left corrupted: _cwd was reassigned to the unreadable directory before the throw (line 187) while _entries still holds the old listing, so the header path and the rows disagree; pressing Enter again re-throws.
findings.evidence: _listEntries (file_picker.dart:115-134) calls `_cwd.listSync(followLinks: false)` (line 116) with no try/catch, and _enterCurrent mutates _cwd before listing inside setState (lines 186-189: `_cwd = e; _listEntries();`). The sibling widget FileBrowser handles exactly this with `on FileSystemException catch` and renders an error row instead (file_browser.dart:273-292), showing the intended pattern; FilePicker never got it. FilePicker's doc only warns that a missing *initialDirectory* throws (file_picker.dart:32-34) — runtime navigation into unreadable dirs is undocumented and unguarded. Same unguarded path via _goUp (line 195-202) for an unreadable parent.

v1.why: file_picker.dart:116 _cwd.listSync has no try/catch; _enterCurrent assigns _cwd=e before listing inside setState (186-189), so PathAccessException leaves _cwd/_entries inconsistent and Enter re-throws (_onKey:218). No catch in InputDispatcher; caught by run_app.dart:654-658 event try/catch and routed to RuntimeErrorReporter → full-screen overlay error banner, app keeps running (runtime_error_overlay.dart:22-34, 107-110) — the finder's runZonedGuarded attribution is the one inaccuracy; user-visible effect stands. FileBrowser handles the same case gracefully (file_browser.dart:288-291); docs only cover initialDirectory (file_picker.dart:32-34). Same unguarded path in _goUp (195-202).
v1.fix_sketch: Mirror FileBrowser: wrap listSync in `on FileSystemException catch`, list into locals and commit _cwd/_entries only on success (or keep an _error field rendered as a row like FileBrowser does). Apply to both _enterCurrent and _goUp; add a test with a mode-000 directory.

v2.why: Traced end-to-end: file_picker.dart:116 listSync unguarded; setState runs fn() bare (framework.dart:346) so _cwd=e (:187) commits before the throw and markNeedsBuild (:347) never runs; escapes _onKey (:218) through InputDispatcher (input_dispatcher.dart:391-392 — no catch anywhere in class; mouse path :103 same); caught only by run_app.dart:654-658 → errorReporter → banner. No guard exists: ErrorBoundary contains layout/paint only (error_boundary.dart:1-9), no test pins throw behavior, no fix in git log. FileBrowser guards the identical call (file_browser.dart:274-291). Corrections: backstop is the event try/catch (not runZonedGuarded), and the banner is bottom-center auto-dismissing, not app-covering (runtime_error_overlay.dart:107-110, 123-130); corruption is recoverable via _goUp. Substantive claims hold.
v2.fix_sketch: Mirror FileBrowser: list into locals inside `try { ... } on FileSystemException catch`, commit _cwd/_entries only on success (or set an _error field rendered as a dim row); apply to _listEntries callers _enterCurrent, _goUp, initState, didUpdateWidget. Test with a chmod-000 temp dir (skip on Windows).

findings.title: MessageList: a streamed append silently re-engages followTail after the app disengaged it, yanking the viewport to the tail
findings.failure_scenario: Agent chat following the tail (default MessageListController; selection sits on the last message). App pauses auto-scroll via the public API `controller.followTail = false` (documented: "freezes in place") or `jumpToIndex`. The next streamed message arrives (new messages list) -> followTail flips back to TRUE and the viewport snaps to the new tail. Verified with the repo test harness: frozen at visible 44..49/follow=false -> after one append visible 45..50, selected 50, follow=true. Same for jumpToIndex(10): visible 10..15 -> after append 45..50, follow=true. Reading history during a stream is impossible via the controller API.
findings.evidence: `_syncSelectionAfterMessageUpdate` (message_list.dart:308-337) runs in didUpdateWidget BEFORE the child ListView pushes the new count, and re-writes the id-preserved selection through `_controller.selectedIndex = nextIndex` (line 342). ListController's cursor-coupling setter (packages/fleury/lib/src/widgets/list_view.dart:166-183) compares against the stale pre-append `_itemCount` (50), so nextIndex 49 == itemCount-1 reads as "cursor landed on tail" -> `_pinToBottom = true` (list_view.dart:172-177). ListView.didUpdateWidget then calls `_handleCountChange(51)` (list_view.dart:200-214): pin && grew -> `_snapToTail()`. A bare core ListView without this wrapper sync freezes correctly (its count-change path re-clamps via `_clampSelection` directly, bypassing the coupling), proving the wrapper's stale-count selection write is the defect. No existing test covers unfollow-then-append (log_region_test.dart:216-266 tests unfollow only).

v1.why: Traced: while following, selection==tail. followTail=false clears pin only (_followsCursor latched, list_view.dart:86). Append: _MessageListState.didUpdateWidget runs _syncSelectionAfterMessageUpdate BEFORE child count push (StatefulElement.update: didUpdateWidget then rebuild, framework.dart:1092-1098) -> _controller.selectedIndex=49 (message_list.dart:342; no-id path :333 too) -> coupling vs stale _itemCount=50 -> 49==50-1 -> _pinToBottom=true (list_view.dart:172-177) -> _handleCountChange(51) -> _snapToTail (list_view.dart:204-206). Violates documented 'freezes in place' (list_view.dart:105). Core-only ListView immune (:209-211 direct write). No unfollow-then-append test.
v1.fix_sketch: In _syncSelectionAfterMessageUpdate, write the preserved selection without the follow-coupling (internal clamp-style write, or capture/restore pinToBottom around it), or defer the sync until after the child pushes the new count (reuse the existing _selectIndexAfterListCountRefresh post-frame path) so on-tail is judged against the fresh count.

v2.fix_sketch: Make the wrapper's selection sync follow-neutral: capture/restore pinToBottom around the `_controller.selectedIndex` writes in _syncSelectionAfterMessageUpdate (or add an internal coupling-free write on ListController), or always route the sync through the existing post-frame `_selectIndexAfterListCountRefresh` path so on-tail is judged against the fresh count (49 != 51-1 → no re-pin). Add the missing unfollow-then-append test.
v2.why: Reproduced with a throwaway widget test (since deleted): default controller, 50 msgs, followTail=false, append → followTail read TRUE (expected false). Chain: followTail=false clears pin but _followsCursor stays latched (list_view.dart:110-120,86). Append: StatefulElement.update runs didUpdateWidget before rebuild (framework.dart:1092-1098) → _syncSelectionAfterMessageUpdate passes its guard (message_list.dart:311, followTail false) → writes selectedIndex=49 (:341-342; no-id path :333) → coupling vs stale _itemCount=50 → onTail → _pinToBottom=true (list_view.dart:172-177) → child _handleCountChange(51) → _snapToTail (:204-206). Violates documented "freezes in place" (:105); core-only ListView freezes correctly (:207-211). Not fixed at HEAD; no test covers unfollow-then-append (existing tests construct followTail:false, so coupling never arms).

findings.title: MessageListController/LogRegionController.jumpToIndex is reverted on the next rebuild: viewport snaps back to the selection left on the old tail
findings.failure_scenario: Log or chat is following the tail (selection = last index, e.g. 49). App implements "jump to entry N" with `controller.jumpToIndex(10)` (the wrappers' only scroll API). The target is shown for exactly one layout; the next rebuild that relayouts the list -- every streamed append, since these widgets setState on each controller/data change -- re-anchors the viewport to keep the stale selection (49) visible. Verified: LogRegion visible 10..15 after jump -> 44..49 after one append (follow stays false but the jump is undone); MessageList additionally re-engages follow (finding 1). "Jump to message during stream" is effectively unusable.
findings.evidence: `jumpToIndex` in both wrappers (log_region.dart:177-181, message_list.dart:149-153) sets followTail=false and issues a pending jump but never moves the selection off the tail. `_RenderListView`/`_RenderLazyListView.performLayout` consume the pending jump for one layout only (list_view.dart:884-889, 1282-1287); every subsequent layout runs the selection-visibility pass (list_view.dart:906-919, 1309-1333): `selected > lastVisible` -> re-anchor so the selection is the bottom-most visible item. `_ListViewBody.updateRenderObject` marks needs-layout on every rebuild (list_view.dart:780-789), so any append triggers the snap-back. PatchReview's own `_jumpDiffToFile` (patch_review.dart:545-555) works around exactly this by setting `selectedIndex` AND `jumpToIndex` -- the two wrappers that don't are broken.

v1.why: Traced: wrapper jumpToIndex (log_region.dart:177-181, message_list.dart:149-153) leaves selection on tail 49. Pending jump consumed for one layout (list_view.dart:1284-1287/884-889) with selection-visibility suppressed only then (!hadPendingJump, :1311/:908). Every rebuild relayouts (updateRenderObject unconditionally markNeedsLayout, :1036-1044/:780-789); append re-clamps but never moves selection, so selected(49)>lastVisible re-anchors viewport to tail selection (:1311-1333). PatchReview._jumpDiffToFile (patch_review.dart:550-551) sets selectedIndex AND jumpToIndex — in-repo workaround proving the wrappers' omission. Core comment's stated intent (jump wins, :881-883) survives one layout only.
v1.fix_sketch: Make wrapper jumpToIndex also move the selection to the target through a non-coupling write (PatchReview pattern: selectedIndex then jumpToIndex), or have the core persist the jump anchor by skipping the selection-visibility re-anchor until the selection actually changes after a jump.

v2.fix_sketch: In both wrapper jumpToIndex methods, also move the selection to the target (PatchReview pattern): `followTail = false; _list.selectedIndex = index; _list.jumpToIndex(index);` — selection at a non-tail index keeps follow disengaged and the selection-visibility pass then preserves the target across relayouts. Alternatively fix in core: persist the jump anchor (skip the selection-visibility re-anchor) until the selection actually changes after a jump.
v2.why: Independently traced; refutation attempts failed. Wrapper jumpToIndex (log_region.dart:177-181, message_list.dart:149-153) leaves selection on the tail (put there by _snapToTail, list_view.dart:204-232). Pending jump is one-shot (:1282-1287); selection-visibility suppression is !hadPendingJump only (:1311); every rebuild relayouts (unconditional markNeedsLayout, :1036-1044); append re-clamps but never moves selection (:207-211), so selected(49)>lastVisible re-anchors viewport to the tail (:1311-1332). MessageList also re-pins follow: _syncSelectionAfterMessageUpdate rewrites selection 49 while old count 50 → coupling (message_list.dart:333-337 → list_view.dart:172-177). No focus gate; no test pins persistence (list_view_test.dart:362-377 checks one layout); patch_review.dart:550-551 is the in-repo workaround. Unchanged at HEAD.

findings.title: Advertised 'q' quit key never works in any sample app — plain printables arrive as TextInputEvent, not KeyEvent
findings.failure_scenario: User runs `dart run packages/samples/bin/samples.dart dashboard` (or files/agent/debug) in any terminal. The usage text (line 65) and every app's on-screen footer ('q quit' — dashboard.dart:264, file_manager.dart:83, agent_tui.dart:196, debug_playground.dart:379) advertise q to quit. Pressing q does nothing (dashboard/files/debug) or just types 'q' into the prompt (agent). Verified empirically in a PTY: dashboard still running 12s after 'q'; only Ctrl+C exits. First-run demo experience: the documented quit key is dead in all four flagship showcases.
findings.evidence: The terminal parser emits plain printable bytes as TextInputEvent, never KeyEvent (packages/fleury/lib/src/terminal/input_parser.dart:269 classic path, :546 Kitty path; KeyEvent doc at input/events.dart:77-82 says KeyEvent is only special keys/Ctrl-chords). runApp's onEvent receives the raw driver event (packages/fleury/lib/src/runtime/run_app.dart:640-641), so `event is KeyEvent && event.char == 'q'` at samples.dart:46-48 can never match a typed q; the dispatcher's synthesized KeyEvent for unclaimed text (input_dispatcher.dart:239-241) only reaches widget-level KeyBindings, never onEvent. run_app.dart:121-125 documents the intended pattern (widget binding + requestExit()). The same broken idiom is shown as teaching code in the file-manager demo content (file_manager.dart:257) and website/examples/lib/registry.dart:1314, and the only test of this path enqueues a synthetic KeyEvent(char:'q') that the real parser never produces (fleury/test/runtime/run_app_signals_test.dart:76-80), masking the bug.

v1.why: Traced: typed 'q' -> TextInputEvent (input_parser.dart:269 classic; :546 Kitty no-modifier). run_app.dart:640-641 hands the RAW driver event to onEvent, so samples.dart:46-48 `event is KeyEvent && event.char=='q'` never matches; the dispatcher-synthesized KeyEvent (input_dispatcher.dart:337) stays inside widget dispatch. Zero KeyBinding/requestExit hits in packages/samples/lib; FleuryApp only binds command shortcuts (app.dart:343-366) and samples pass none. Footers (dashboard.dart:264, file_manager.dart:83, agent_tui.dart:196, debug_playground.dart:379) and samples.dart:65 all advertise q. Ctrl+C works via 0x03->KeyEvent + run_app.dart:630-637.
v1.fix_sketch: Follow the intended pattern (run_app.dart:121-125): give each sample a widget-level KeyBinding('q') -> requestExit() (agent app must scope it so a focused prompt keeps typing 'q'). Do NOT just match TextInputEvent in onEvent — onEvent ignores dispatchResult, so that would exit while typing 'q' into the agent prompt. Also fix the teaching snippet at file_manager.dart:257 and website/examples registry, and make run_app_signals_test drive a TextInputEvent so the real parser behavior is pinned.

v2.why: Traced end-to-end: typed 'q' → TextInputEvent (input_parser.dart:269 classic, :546 Kitty; parser never emits modifier-free KeyEvent(char)). run_app.dart:641 hands the raw event to onEvent, and KeyEvent/TextInputEvent are sibling final classes of sealed TuiEvent (events.dart:84,:143), so samples.dart:46-48 never matches. Dispatcher-synthesized KeyEvent (input_dispatcher.dart:239-241) reaches only KeyBindings — and none exist: samples/lib has zero (file_manager.dart:257 is a string constant), FleuryApp binds only app.commands (app.dart:442-450, samples pass none), globalBindings default [] (run_app.dart:249). Advertised at samples.dart:65 plus all four footers. Ironically Ctrl+Q/Alt+Q do quit (char=='q', modifiers unchecked); plain q does not.
v2.fix_sketch: Follow the documented pattern (run_app.dart:121-125): add a KeyBinding('q') → requestExit() per sample, scoped so the agent app's focused prompt still types 'q' (a bare TextInputEvent match in onEvent would exit mid-typing). Also correct the teaching snippet in file_manager.dart's _mainDart constant and website/examples registry, and make run_app_signals_test drive TextInputEvent('q') instead of a parser-impossible KeyEvent(char:'q').

findings.title: _globalKeyClaims false positive: a GlobalKey inflated in pass 1 of a flushBuild and legitimately moved in pass 2 of the same flush throws a spurious 'Duplicate GlobalKey detected' StateError
findings.failure_scenario: A stateful ancestor builds Keyed(key: gk) bare; Keyed's initState registers with the ancestor which calls setState (the autofocus / controller-registration pattern — setState-during-build is explicitly supported, framework.dart:797-803). Pass 1 of the flush inflates Keyed (claim recorded gk->parent). Pass 2: the ancestor rebuilds and now wraps the child: WrapB(child: Keyed(key: gk)). updateChild deactivates the old keyed element FIRST, then WrapB's build re-inflates gk — at no instant are two elements with the key mounted — but _claimGlobalKey still holds pass 1's claim and throws StateError 'Duplicate GlobalKey detected in the widget tree... used to inflate two widgets in one build pass'. This is a plain StateError, so it fires in release too; the flush aborts and the subtree renders blank/error until an unrelated setState. Verified with a runnable repro (tester.pump after advancing the stage): exact error produced at framework.dart:1239.
findings.evidence: packages/fleury/lib/src/widgets/framework.dart:1234 — _globalKeyClaims persists 'across all of a flushBuild's passes' and _claimGlobalKey (:1236-1248) throws on any second claim; claims are recorded on every inflate (:889) but never released when the claimed element or its enclosing subtree is deactivated later in the same flush, so 'inflated twice in one flush' is conflated with 'mounted twice at once'. The check that PR #103 (d4af9c7) made release-safe is correct for the steal-back storm it targets, but over-fires on this legitimate sequential move.

v1.why: Reproduced at HEAD: two-stage host (pass 1 inflates Keyed(gk) bare, initState's setState triggers pass 2 which wraps it) threw 'Bad state: Duplicate GlobalKey detected in the widget tree' from _claimGlobalKey (:1236-1248) inside one tester.pump(), though updateChild deactivated the old element (:872) before WrapB re-inflated it — never two mounted at once. _globalKeyClaims is cleared only at flush boundaries (:1346/:1353), never per-key on deactivation, contradicting its own doc ':1227 a key another parent STILL holds'. Mid-flush cross-element setState is explicitly supported (:1337-1339 sibling-setState; State.setState :339-348 has no build-phase guard). Plain StateError, so release too; narrow trigger -> P2.
v1.fix_sketch: Release a key's claim when its element is deactivated (e.g. in _deactivateChild/updateChild), or before throwing verify the existing claimant still holds a mounted element for that key (check key._element lifecycle/parent) and overwrite the claim if not.

findings.title: ListView.builder renders permanently blank when the controller instance is swapped without an itemCount change
