findings.failure_scenario: A ListView.builder(itemCount: 100, controller: a) is rebuilt with a different controller instance b (per-tab controllers, or removing the controller argument so the state creates a fallback). didUpdateWidget replaces the controller but only calls _handleCountChange when effectiveItemCount changed, so the fresh controller keeps _itemCount == 0. _RenderLazyListView.performLayout reads count from controller._itemCount, sees 0, unmounts every mounted item and renders empty — and since nothing else ever writes the count for the lazy path, the list stays blank on every subsequent frame until the itemCount property itself changes. Reproduced empirically: after the swap the rendered frame is entirely blank.
findings.evidence: list_view.dart:470-479 swaps _controller without pushing the count (contrast initState at :448 which does `_controller._itemCount = count`); :486-491 only calls _handleCountChange when newCount != oldWidget.effectiveItemCount. The lazy render object uses the controller as the count source of truth: :1272 `final count = _controller._itemCount;` and :1274-1279 treats count==0 as empty (unmounts all, visibleRange=null) without writing the count back. The eager path is unaffected because _RenderListView derives count from _children.length (:876, :920). No existing test covers controller swapping (grep of test/widgets/list_view_test.dart).

v1.why: Traced: didUpdateWidget swaps in a fresh ListController (list_view.dart:470-480) whose _itemCount defaults to 0 (:73); _handleCountChange (:201-202) only runs when effectiveItemCount changed (:486-491). All other _itemCount writes: initState :448, eager render :876/:920 (self-heals from _children.length), lazy render :1344 which writes back the value read at :1272 and is unreachable when the empty branch :1274-1279 returns. _LazyListBody.updateRenderObject (:1040) installs the new controller; performLayout reads count=0, unmounts everything, renders blank permanently. Violates the documented contract 'widget updates itemCount before each build' (:63-65).
v1.fix_sketch: In didUpdateWidget, after installing the replacement controller, push the count: `_controller._itemCount = widget.effectiveItemCount;` (and re-default/clamp selection as initState does) before the count-change check — or have _RenderLazyListView take itemCount from the widget like the eager path derives it from children.

findings.title: Lazy ListView leaks mounted item elements: the end-of-layout unmount sweep only covers priorlyMounted, missing items mounted during the layout's anchor probe and pre-jump first walk
findings.failure_scenario: ListView.builder(itemCount: 1000, controller: ListController(pinToBottom: true)) in a 10-row viewport (a chat/log): first layout walks from anchor 0 and mounts items 0..9, then the selection (999) forces an anchor recompute; newlyVisible/_activeByIndex are cleared and the final window becomes 990..999. The sweep iterates only priorlyMounted (empty on first layout), so items 0..9 plus the probe boundary item 989 stay mounted in _LazyListElement._mountedChildren forever — never painted, but rebuilt via itemBuilder on every list rebuild (measured 21 itemBuilder calls per rebuild instead of 10) and holding their element/render subtrees in memory. Every subsequent far selection jump (End key, programmatic selectedIndex) leaks one more boundary item (measured indices 240 and 489 accumulating after Home/jump/End cycles). Long-running list UIs accumulate dead mounted subtrees: unbounded memory growth plus per-rebuild CPU tax.
findings.evidence: performLayout captures priorlyMounted from _activeByIndex before layout (:1298) and sweeps only it (:1337-1341 `for (final i in priorlyMounted) { if (!newlyVisible.contains(i)) element.disposeChild(i); }`). _anchorThatEndsAt (:1389-1406) mounts items via element.createChild — including the boundary item that fails the fit test — but never records them in newlyVisible; the re-walk path (:1313-1319) clears newlyVisible and _activeByIndex, erasing the first walk's mount record while its comment claims the leftover sweep will clean up probe mounts. Leaked entries are rebuilt every rebuild by _LazyListElement.performRebuild (:1103-1120), which iterates all _mountedChildren. Empirically confirmed with itemBuilder call tracing at HEAD.

v1.why: Traced: priorlyMounted snapshots _activeByIndex (:1298, empty on first layout); first walk mounts anchor-0 window (:1365-1379); pinToBottom sets selection=count-1 in initState (:453-454) so selected>lastVisible triggers _anchorThatEndsAt (:1389-1406), which mounts probe items incl. the failing boundary without recording them; the re-walk clears newlyVisible AND _activeByIndex (:1315-1319), erasing the first-walk record, while the sweep iterates only priorlyMounted (:1337-1341). Leaked indices stay in _LazyListElement._mountedChildren and _adopted/_indexByObject forever (later priorlyMounted never contains them), and performRebuild (:1103-1120) rebuilds every mounted entry via itemBuilder on each rebuild. The comment at :1320-1322/:1386-1388 promising sweep cleanup is wrong.
v1.fix_sketch: Make the sweep authoritative over actual mounts, not the prior active set: sweep `element._mountedChildren.keys` (anything not in newlyVisible gets disposeChild), or record probe/first-walk mounts into a per-layout mounted set that the sweep unions with priorlyMounted.

findings.title: Key sequences whose continuation step is a bare printable never complete while a text field is focused; the held leader key is silently dropped (contradicting the documented cancel+redispatch) and the continuation char is inserted into the field
findings.failure_scenario: KeyBindings([.ctrl.x.b -> switchBuffer, .ctrl.x -> direct]) around a focused TextInput (the .ctrl.x.b chord is key_bindings.dart's own doc example, line 103). Press Ctrl+X: pending sequence starts. Press 'b': it arrives as TextInputEvent and _dispatchText delivers it to the claimant FIRST — the field consumes it — then _clearPending() discards the held Ctrl+X with no replay. Verified at HEAD: switchBuffer fired 0 times, the direct Ctrl+X binding fired 0 times, and controller.text == 'b' (the chord's continuation char corrupts the field); baseline without a focused field fires the sequence. The hint bar still advertises such chords as live while typing, because KeyChord.isShadowedByTextInput (key_bindings.dart:321) inspects only step 0 (Ctrl+X is modified, so not shadowed).
findings.evidence: _dispatchText (input_dispatcher.dart 232-247): `_deliverText` runs before any pending-sequence handling, violating the file's own precedence doc ('1. PENDING SEQUENCE: complete or cancel-and-redispatch', lines 4-13); on a claimed result it does `if (_pending != null) _clearPending(); return` — dropping held events, while the method's doc comment (227-231) promises 'cancel + redispatch the original key first ... then deliver the text'. Every other pending-breaking path (_dispatchKeyEvent 191-196, _dispatchPaste 255-259, _dispatchComposition 267-271) replays held events via direct-only dispatch; only the claimed-text path loses them.

v1.why: Reachable: default keymap maps Ctrl+X to cut (text_keymap.dart:120-122) but _copyOrCutSelection returns ignored on empty selection (text_input.dart:1159), so Ctrl+X reaches the ancestor KeyBindings and _startPending arms (input_dispatcher.dart:398-400; direct match deferred, 357-377). 'b' arrives as TextInputEvent: _dispatchText (232-237) delivers to the claimant first (onTextInput inserts+handled, text_input.dart:1369-1376) then _clearPending() with no replay, contradicting the file-top precedence doc (lines 4-13) and its own doc (227-231); all sibling paths replay held events (191-196, 255-259, 267-271, 466-476). isShadowedByTextInput (key_bindings.dart:321) checks step 0 only, so the hint bar keeps advertising the chord.
v1.fix_sketch: In _dispatchText, handle the pending sequence before delivery per the documented order: _cancelPendingAndRedispatchHeld() (replaying held via _dispatchPlain allowSequenceStart:false) before _deliverText - or first offer the printable to pending.tryComplete so text-continuation sequences can actually complete while a field is focused, then fall through to delivery.

findings.title: Inline-image placement severs wide-glyph pairs (no _evictWideNeighbors), causing cursor desync that garbles the rest of the row on probed-narrow terminals
findings.failure_scenario: A Stack paints CJK text, then an Image sibling whose left edge lands on the continuation half of a wide glyph: `writeText((10,1),'你好 AFTER')` then `writeImage((11,1), w:4, h:1)`. The overlay-marking loop overwrites the continuation cell at col 11 but leaves the leading '你' at col 10 intact — violating the buffer's own invariants 2/3 (every other write path evicts wide neighbors). The renderer then emits '你' (terminal advances 2 columns, cursor at 12) but bookkeeps `cursorCol = 11` because `isWide` sees an overlay (not continuation) at col+1. Repro at HEAD emits `ESC[2;11H你ESC[4CAFTER`: on the terminal the CUF 4 runs from col 12, so 'AFTER' paints at cols 16-20 instead of 15-19 — one column right, with stale content left at col 15 — i.e., the whole row right of the image garbles. This desync is masked only when `ambiguousCharsAreWide` is true (per-glyph cursor invalidation); after the startup cursor-position probe confirms narrow — described in code/docs as the common case — the compact-run mode is active and the garble is real. Additionally the emitted '你' right half visually spills into the image's first column on every terminal.
findings.evidence: cell_buffer.dart:693-699 `_recordImagePlacement` marks the region with `_cells[base + c] = const Cell.overlay();` directly, never calling `_evictWideNeighbors` (cell_buffer.dart:736-747), unlike `_writeGraphemeAt` which always does. ansi_renderer.dart:369-373 computes `isWide` by checking `next.atColRow(col+1,row).role == CellRole.continuation` — false for the overlay cell — so `cursorCol = col + 1` while the terminal advanced 2; the following same-row `_cursorMove` (ansi_renderer.dart:489-492) emits a relative CUF from the wrong tracked column. Verified by running the repro against HEAD: cell roles show leading '你' at col 10 followed by overlay at col 11, output bytes `ESC[2;11H你ESC[4CAFTER` with `AnsiRenderer(ambiguousCharsAreWide: false)`.

v1.why: Reproduced at HEAD. _recordImagePlacement stamps Cell.overlay() raw (cell_buffer.dart:693-699); _evictWideNeighbors is called only from grapheme writes (cell_buffer.dart:432-443). writeText('你好 AFTER') then writeImage((11,1),w4,h1) left leading '你' at col 10 with overlay at col 11 (invariant broken). AnsiRenderer isWide requires continuation at col+1 (ansi_renderer.dart:369-373) -> false -> cursorCol tracked +1 while terminal advances +2; with ambiguousCharsAreWide:false no width-pin invalidation (line 400) so same-row CUF is computed from the wrong column: emitted exactly ESC[2;11H你ESC[4CAFTER, shifting AFTER one column right. Needs image-over-wide-glyph overlap and probed-narrow mode: P2.
v1.fix_sketch: In _recordImagePlacement's overlay-marking loop, call _evictWideNeighbors(c, r) (or at least at the region's left and right edges) before stamping Cell.overlay(), matching every other write path.

findings.title: SGR mouse decoder misreports horizontal wheel (buttons 6/7) as vertical scroll and extended buttons 8-11 as left/middle/right clicks
findings.failure_scenario: App runs with TerminalMode(mouse: true) (all bundled samples do). (a) Two-finger horizontal trackpad swipe in kitty/iTerm2/WezTerm sends SGR wheel-left/right reports CSI<66;x;yM / CSI<67;x;yM; _emitMouse sees cb & 64 != 0 and derives direction from cb & 1 only, so 66 → MouseEventKind.scrollUp and 67 → scrollDown — a horizontal gesture scrolls a ListView vertically. (b) Pressing a mouse side/thumb button (SGR button 8, cb=128) over a Button widget: cb & 64 == 0 and cb & 3 == 0, so it becomes MouseEvent(down, left) and the 'm' release becomes up-left — verified via harness: feeding \x1B[<128;3;4M yields MouseEvent(down left @2,3) — so a back-navigation thumb button activates whatever is under the cursor as if left-clicked.
findings.evidence: input_parser.dart:642-656 treats every cb with bit 64 as a two-direction wheel (cb & 1 chooses up/down), but the SGR encoding defines 64+2/64+3 as wheel-left/wheel-right; input_parser.dart:658-663 masks cb & 3 for the button without checking bit 128 (buttons 8-11), so 128..131 alias to left/middle/right. events.dart MouseEventKind has no horizontal-scroll variant, so 66/67 must be dropped (or given new kinds) rather than mapped to vertical scroll. Confirmed empirically with the parser harness at HEAD; git history shows no prior fix touching _emitMouse button bits.

v1.why: input_parser.dart:642-655: any cb with bit 64 is treated as vertical wheel with direction from cb&1, so SGR wheel-left/right (cb 66/67 per xterm ctlseqs; emitted by kitty/iTerm2/WezTerm for horizontal trackpad swipes) become scrollUp/scrollDown. Lines 658-663 mask cb&3 with no bit-128 check, so buttons 8-11 (cb 128-131, e.g. mouse back/forward) alias to left/middle/right down+up clicks. MouseEventKind (lib/src/input/events.dart:202) has no horizontal variants. SGR 1006 enabled by TerminalMode(mouse:true) (terminal_sequences.dart:23-25); samples enable it (packages/samples/bin/samples.dart:44).
v1.fix_sketch: In _emitMouse, when cb&64 != 0 switch on cb&3: 0->scrollUp, 1->scrollDown, 2/3 -> drop (or add scrollLeft/scrollRight kinds + wire to horizontal scrollables). For cb&128 != 0, map to new MouseButton.back/forward (or MouseButton.none) instead of falling into the cb&3 left/middle/right decode.

findings.title: Second Fleury session in the same process crashes at enter(): re-listening the process-global stdin after a previous restore() throws StateError
findings.failure_scenario: A CLI runs runApp (picker TUI), the app exits normally — restore() awaits _stdinSubscription.cancel() (posix_driver.dart:851-854) — then the same process calls runApp again with a fresh PosixTerminalDriver (e.g. menu → app → menu). enter() enables raw mode and writes the alt-screen/enter sequences, then _stdin.listen(...) at line 342 throws 'Bad state: Stream has already been listened to' because dart:io's stdin is a cached process-global single-subscription stream: once cancelled it can never be listened to again (verified empirically: stdin.listen → cancel → listen throws). The second session never starts; runApp's zone treats it as a pre-mount fatal, cleanup restores the terminal, and the run fails with an obscure StateError. Same pattern in windows_driver.dart:114.
findings.evidence: posix_driver.dart:342 (_stdinSubscription = _stdin.listen(...)) after raw-mode/enter-sequence writes at lines 324-340; restore() cancels rather than pauses the subscription (lines 851-854), and _stdin is the dart:io singleton when no override is passed (line 46). Scratch test: 'stdin.listen → await cancel → stdin.listen' prints 'Bad state: Stream has already been listened to.' on Dart at HEAD SDK. The TerminalDriver contract only says enter is called 'once at startup' per driver (terminal_driver.dart:71); nothing prevents or documents against a second sequential session per process, and a fresh driver instance still hits the shared-singleton limit.
also: lens-lifecycle: Second runApp in the same process always fails: stdin is a single-subscription stream and PosixTerminalDriver.enter re-listens after the first session cancelled it

v1.why: posix_driver.dart:46 uses the dart:io singleton stdin; restore() awaits _stdinSubscription.cancel() (852); a second session's enter() re-listens at 342 after raw mode + enter sequences (324-340). I empirically reproduced the load-bearing SDK behavior on this machine: stdin.listen -> cancel -> listen throws 'Bad state: Stream has already been listened to.' The handoff path deliberately uses pause/resume instead (731-735, 776), so cancel-at-restore makes any later session impossible. runApp's finally->cleanup->restore (run_app.dart:1263-1265, 774) does restore the terminal, so the second run fails with an obscure StateError but the terminal is not left broken. Same pattern windows_driver.dart:114. No doc or guard covers sequential sessions (terminal_driver.dart:71 is per-driver).
v1.fix_sketch: Never cancel the process-global stdin subscription: keep one process-lifetime subscription (e.g. a static broadcast/relay created on first listen) that drivers attach/detach from, or pause() in restore() and resume() in enter() when _stdin is the global singleton (cancel remains fine for injected stdinOverride streams). At minimum, throw a descriptive StateError from enter() explaining the one-session-per-process limit.

findings.title: Startup-input overflow thrown after mount leaves runApp's future permanently unresolved and silently swallows the error (terminal restored, process exits 0 with no diagnostic)
findings.failure_scenario: App starts with hot reload enabled (the default); a >1MiB paste or a >4096-event stdin burst arrives after `enter()` returns but before `activateDriverEvents()` runs (the mount + `HotReloadController.attach` await window — tens of ms with a VM service attached; any driver event emitted during first build also lands here, which is how the repro triggers it deterministically). `_StartupEventBuffer` records `overflowError`; `activateDriverEvents()` (run_app.dart:676/684, called at :1260 inside the guarded try) throws it. The `finally` runs `cleanup()` — disposing frameDriver, errorReporter, runtime, restoring the terminal — then the error escapes the async zone body and reaches the `runZonedGuarded` handler. There `errorReporter.report()` is a silent no-op (disposed guard, runtime_error_overlay.dart:74) so `reportingFailed` stays false, and `frameDriver.rootElement` is still non-null because `FrameDriver.dispose()` never clears `_rootElement` (frame_driver.dart:493-496), so the fatal test at run_app.dart:1298-1301 classifies the error as survivable and does nothing. `done` is never completed (the only success completion at :1266 was skipped), so `runApp()`'s future never resolves: the caller's `await runApp(...)` and everything after it (exit-code mapping, host shutdown) silently never run — the process either hangs (if other event sources are live) or drains and exits 0 with zero output. Repro script confirms: 'terminal restored: true' then the future stays pending and the StateError is printed nowhere. This directly violates the buffer's own contract ('fails startup loudly', run_app.dart:139) and the cleanup contract ('must not ... keep [runApp] pending forever', run_app.dart:186), and the existing tests (run_app_test.dart:399-441) only cover overflow during enter(), which hits the pre-try check at :906-907 and correctly fails loudly.
findings.evidence: run_app.dart:674-695 `activateDriverEvents` throws the overflow inside the try at :1260; :1263-1265 `finally { await cleanup(); }` disposes everything before the error reaches the zone handler; :1298-1305 the handler's fatal criteria (`reportingFailed || renderUnrecoverable || rootElement == null || isStorming`) are all false post-cleanup because frame_driver.dart:493-496 `dispose()` sets `_disposed` but never nulls `_rootElement` (getter :243), and runtime_error_overlay.dart:74 `report()` returns silently when `_disposed`; no other code path completes `done`. Confirmed by executing a repro (fake driver + a widget whose build emits a 1MiB+1 PasteEvent): output was `terminal restored: true` followed by `BUG CONFIRMED: runApp future never resolved`, process exit 0, no error text anywhere. Introduced by commit bb94ccc ('Harden runtime input and teardown', 2026-07-16); nothing at HEAD fixes it.
also: lens-concurrency: runApp's returned future never resolves when an error escapes the guarded body after mount (e.g. the startup input-buffer overflow), leaving the process hung after teardown
lens-lifecycle: runApp's returned future never settles (and the error is silently swallowed) when the guarded body throws after mount — e.g. the startup input-overflow guard

v1.why: Traced full chain at HEAD: buffered events (run_app.dart:877-885) overflow during the post-enter mount window (await HotReloadController.attach :1245, hot reload default-on :244); activateDriverEvents (:1260) throws at :676/:684 inside try (:1085); finally cleanup (:1264) disposes reporter (:765) and frameDriver (:737); rethrow skips done.complete (:1266); zone handler (:1268): report() no-ops when disposed (runtime_error_overlay.dart:74), FrameDriver.dispose never nulls _rootElement (frame_driver.dart:493-496), frameDriver never nulled (:1171 only assignment) — fatal test :1298-1301 all false, so done (:530) never settles and the error prints nowhere. Tests :399-441 only cover onEnter overflow hitting :906-907 (frameDriver null → fatal). Violates contracts at :139 and :186.
v1.fix_sketch: Append a catch to the guarded body's try so an error escaping after mount completes done directly: `catch (e, s) { if (!done.isCompleted) done.completeError(e, s); rethrow-or-not }` (cleanup already ran in finally). Additionally harden the zone handler: treat `cleanupFuture != null` (teardown already started) as fatal, and fall back to stderr when the reporter is disposed. Optionally null _rootElement in FrameDriver.dispose(). Add a test injecting overflow via an event emitted during first build / the attach window.

findings.title: Browser resize of a served session blanks the visible grid for a full network round trip (in-flight old-size plans then repaint only scattered dirty rows on the blank grid)
findings.failure_scenario: Served session over a real network (e.g. 100ms RTT), app idle or animating. User drags the browser window edge; each ResizeObserver tick that changes integer cols/rows calls components.surface.resize(next) which discards every retained row and recreates them EMPTY, so the screen goes blank until the server's full-repaint plan for the new size arrives one RTT later — during a drag the grid is blank essentially the whole time. Worse, an in-flight non-full plan built for the OLD size arriving in that window makes DomGridSurface.present() resize the (new-size, blank) surface BACK to the old size — blanking again — and then paints only that plan's dirty rows, so the user sees a mostly-blank frame with scattered repainted rows until the server's resize response lands. Self-heals (run_app.dart:598-603 forces a full repaint on every ResizeEvent), but the blank/garbage flash is user-visible on every resize, proportional to RTT.
findings.evidence: wire_frame_source.dart:307-339 (_handleObservedResize calls components.surface.resize(next, metrics: box) at ~329 and deliberately does NOT reset or repaint from _mirror — comment at 335-338); dom_grid_surface.dart:102-120 (resize() with a changed size clears _rows and replaceChildren's the root with freshly created EMPTY row divs — content discarded); dom_grid_surface.dart:63 (present(): `if (plan.size != _size) resize(plan.size)` resizes the surface back to an in-flight plan's old size, blanking again, then only plan.dirtyRowModels rows are painted). The mirror (_mirror) still holds full old-size content but nothing repaints the DOM from it after the local resize — _resyncFromMirror (wire_frame_source.dart:446-456) exists but is only invoked on apply failures. Tests (wire_frame_source_test.dart:301-418) assert RESIZE is sent and surface.size updates, but never assert content preservation across resize.

v1.why: Traced: metrics observer -> _handleObservedResize (wire_frame_source.dart:329) -> DomGridSurface.resize; sizeChanged clears _rows and replaceChildren's the root with EMPTY divs (dom_grid_surface.dart:110-118). No repaint from _mirror follows (only caret/imageOverlay/ResizeFrame, :330-338), so the grid stays blank until the server's plan arrives one RTT later (self-heal via run_app.dart:598-602 forceFullRepaint). In-flight old-size diff plan: plan.size==_mirror.size skips reset (:494), applyRemotePlan returns old-size touched-rows-only plan (plan_adapter.dart:14-21), present() resizes back to old size then paints only those rows (dom_grid_surface.dart:63) = mostly-blank frame. Tests never pin content preservation. Transient+self-healing, invisible on localhost -> P2.
v1.fix_sketch: After surface.resize(next) in _handleObservedResize, repaint the overlap region locally: copy _mirror into a next-sized CellBuffer (or keep a size-matched paint buffer) and present a full-repaint plan at the new size; also drop (or re-project) in-flight plans whose size != current surface size instead of letting present() resize the surface backward.

findings.title: ExcludeSemantics is defeated on serve/web: the coverage fallback re-exposes the excluded subtree's visible text to agents and assistive tech
findings.failure_scenario: App wraps still-rendered content in ExcludeSemantics(excluding: true) — the documented way to hide a closed-but-mounted drawer or 'the background behind a modal an agent shouldn't touch' from 'a screen reader, the web accessible-DOM mirror, and an MCP agent' (semantics.dart:1420-1427). On any serve/web/embed session the FrameSemanticsPipeline runs applySemanticTextFallback (coverageFallback defaults to true, frame_semantics_pipeline.dart:71,312-324) over the painted buffer: the excluded subtree contributes no semantic nodes, so its painted cells are 'uncovered non-whitespace buffer text' and the pass synthesizes SemanticNode(id: __fleury_text_fallback_<row>_<col>, role: text, label/value: <the excluded text>) straight from the cell buffer. REPRODUCED at HEAD: ExcludeSemantics(child: Text('agent-hidden-secret')) yields a semantic tree without the text, but applySemanticTextFallback on the rendered buffer returns fallbackNodeCount:1 with label 'agent-hidden-secret'. Result: the exact content the app asked to hide is shipped to the browser mirror and MCP agents as readable text nodes every flush (actions are not exposed, but the content is), silently contradicting the API contract; exclusion only actually works on the terminal surface, where no coverage pass runs.
findings.evidence: semantic_coverage.dart:104-117 builds fallback nodes from buffer.textInRange for every uncovered painted run with no knowledge of exclusion (excluded subtrees leave no bounds behind to suppress the region — _ExcludeSemanticsElement simply skips children in visitSemanticChildren, semantics.dart:1490-1494). The pipeline applies it to every presented tree on structured surfaces (frame_semantics_pipeline.dart:312-324, 'the visual surface is aria-hidden on every browser path'). No test covers the interaction (ExcludeSemantics appears only in semantics_test.dart:1121; semantic_coverage_test.dart has no exclusion case), and git history shows ExcludeSemantics (3b9f227) predates 'serve gains the coverage fallback' (58252d1) — the leak became reachable when the two features were composed.

v1.why: Traced. _ExcludeSemanticsElement contributes no node and skips children (semantics.dart:1428-1429, 1491-1494) → excluded painted cells have no covering bounds; _coveredCells (semantic_coverage.dart:202-232) marks them uncovered (container roles excluded from coverage anyway, :255-282); applySemanticTextFallback (:104-117) synthesizes SemanticNode(role:text, label/value = buffer.textInRange) for them, appended to the presented root (:132-137). Pipeline applies it every structured-surface flush (frame_semantics_pipeline.dart:71,312-324; run_app.dart:1161 uses the default). 'semanticFallback' state is only counted by fleury_web instrumentation, never filtered from presentation. Directly contradicts the doc promise (semantics.dart:1421-1425: hides from web mirror + MCP agents). Only test is semantics_test.dart:1121 (tree collection); no coverage-composition test. P2: masked when an ancestor readable non-container node spans the region; content is still humanly visible; actions not exposed.
v1.fix_sketch: Make exclusion leave a coverage shadow: have _ExcludeSemanticsElement (when excluding) contribute an invisible bounds-bearing node (e.g. role with no text, marked state {'semanticExcluded': true}) or record its painted bounds into SemanticTree metadata, and teach _coveredCells/applySemanticTextFallback to treat those regions as covered without emitting fallback text. Add a test composing ExcludeSemantics + applySemanticTextFallback asserting the excluded text never appears in the presented tree, and document the interaction in both doc comments.

findings.title: A canceled task later flips its public status to failed when the interrupted runner throws a non-TaskCanceled error
findings.failure_scenario: User cancels a running task; controller.status becomes canceled and the awaited future resolves TaskResult.canceled. The runner, torn down by that cancellation, subsequently throws an ordinary exception (typical: a killed helper process wrapper throwing on broken pipe, an aborted HTTP request throwing, a closed-stream error) instead of TaskCanceled. The catch block then overwrites the already-canceled state: status flips canceled -> failed, error/stackTrace are set, a failed event is recorded, and listeners are notified — TaskStatusView and status bars that showed 'canceled' suddenly re-render 'failed: ...' with an error banner for a task the user intentionally canceled. Verified by probe: after cancel(), awaited result=canceled but controller.status later = failed with the late error.
findings.evidence: TaskController._run's success path explicitly guards against overwriting a canceled state (`if (_cancelRequested || _status == TaskStatus.canceled)` at task.dart:372-375), but the generic catch path (task.dart:394-406) only checks `_isCurrent(runId)` — cancel() does not bump _runId, so a same-run late error passes the guard and sets `_status = TaskStatus.failed`, records TaskEventKind.failed and calls notifyListeners() (task.dart:399-405). The asymmetry with the success-path guard shows the canceled state was meant to be sticky.

v1.why: cancel() (task.dart:309-318) sets status canceled without bumping _runId, so a later non-TaskCanceled throw from the torn-down runner passes the catch's only guard `if (!_isCurrent(runId))` (:395) and overwrites _status = failed, sets error/stackTrace, records TaskEventKind.failed and notifies (:399-405); TaskStatusView then renders failed + validationError (:670-672). The success path's explicit sticky-cancel guard (:372-375) and the TaskCanceled path (:383-393) show canceled was meant to stick — only the generic catch lacks it. Awaited future stays canceled (completer completed at :314). Note the built-in process runner usually converts to TaskCanceled via checkCancellation (process_task.dart:177) first, so this mainly hits custom runners whose teardown throws (broken pipe, aborted request) — edge sequence, hence P2.
v1.fix_sketch: In the generic catch, mirror the success-path guard: if (_cancelRequested || _status == TaskStatus.canceled) complete canceled and skip the failed transition.

findings.title: Retargeting an Animation from a listener on its settling tick silently snaps instead of animating; the new TickerFuture completes instantly
findings.failure_scenario: Animation at 0.0, `a.to(1.0, curve: Curves.linear, duration: 100ms)` in flight, with a chaining listener `a.addListener(() { if (a.value == 1.0) a.to(0.0, curve: ..., duration: 100ms); })` (the curve path lands on exactly 1.0 on the settling tick, so the condition fires precisely then). Trigger: the settling scheduler tick. Outcome (empirically verified at HEAD): the value teleports 1.0 -> 0.0 within that same tick, isMoving is false immediately, the requested 100ms animation never plays a single frame, and the new TickerFuture completes naturally as if it had run. Same corruption applies to loop()/run() issued from a listener on the settling tick (loop additionally starts with from/target swapped by the mirror re-arm).
findings.evidence: _tick (animation.dart:374-422) computes `settled` from the OLD engine (lines 381-383), then calls `_notify()` (line 386) which runs user listeners synchronously; a listener's `to()` re-arms the engine and installs a new `_active` future via _beginRequest/_arm, but `_tick` then consults the stale `settled` (line 388 `if (!settled) return;`), falls into the Done branch (lines 415-421) and runs `_snapToTarget()` — which copies the freshly-retargeted `_target` into `_position` — then `_ticker!.stop()` and `f?.completeNaturally()` on the NEW future. Nothing re-checks whether `_notify()` re-armed the animation.

v1.why: _tick computes settled from the old engine (animation.dart:381-383), calls _notify() (386) which synchronously runs listeners, then uses stale settled (388). A listener's to() installs a new _active (_beginRequest 464-471) and re-arms (_arm 509), but the Done branch (415-421) then runs _snapToTarget() — copying the NEW _target into _position (teleport) — stops the ticker, and completeNaturally()s the NEW future. Curves.linear lands position exactly on target on the settling tick (_stepCurve 444-454), so a value==end chaining listener deterministically hits it; a loop() re-issued there enters the stale mirror branch (391-395) with from/target swapped.
v1.fix_sketch: In _tick, detect re-entrant re-arm: capture the request identity before _notify (e.g. final activeBefore = _active, or a generation counter bumped in _beginRequest) and after _notify return early if it changed, so the new request plays out on subsequent ticks instead of being consumed by the stale Done/loop/queue branches.

findings.title: FleuryTester.renderToString drops overlay (inline-image) cells entirely, shifting every later column left — image goldens encode wrong geometry and cannot detect text/image overlap
findings.failure_scenario: Test with images: InlineImageSupport.placements pumps Row([SizedBox(w:6, child: Image(...)), Text('HI')]) and snapshots via renderToString/matchesGolden. Buffer ground truth (verified at HEAD): row roles are OOOOOOLL — six overlay cells at cols 0-5, 'H' at col 6. Snapshot output: 'HI' at string column 0. The image's 6-column footprint vanishes from the snapshot, so (a) the golden records the caption at the wrong position, and (b) a regression that moves the text INTO the image region (cols 0-5) produces the identical snapshot as the correct layout — the harness silently green-lights the overlap. Any layout differing only in image width also snapshots identically.
findings.evidence: renderToString's switch (fleury_tester.dart:547-559) writes emptyMark for CellRole.empty and the grapheme for leading, but `case CellRole.continuation: case CellRole.overlay: break;` (lines 552-558) writes nothing for overlay — directly contradicting its own comment on lines 555-557 ('the snapshot is text-only so we treat them as emptyMark'). Overlay cells are stamped per-column by image placement (rendering/cell_buffer.dart:693-699 `_cells[base + c] = const Cell.overlay()`), so unlike continuation cells (whose leading cell contributed a wide grapheme) they contribute zero width, shifting all subsequent columns. Introduced in commit d6a749e (PR7+PR8, images-become-placements) and not fixed by PR #116.

v1.why: renderToString's switch (fleury_tester.dart:547-558) shares continuation's no-write break for CellRole.overlay, contradicting its own comment ('treat them as emptyMark'). Overlay cells are stamped per column (cell_buffer.dart _recordImagePlacement: _cells[base+c] = const Cell.overlay()) from the Image widget's writeImageWithId (fleury_widgets/lib/src/image.dart:831) under InlineImageSupport.placements — a tester constructor option (fleury_tester.dart:77). No leading cell covers them, so each overlay column contributes zero string width, shifting later columns left; text overlapping the image region snapshots identically. goldens.dart:5-7 shows matchesGolden fed by renderToString, so goldens inherit it. Latest tester commits (ab7baf0 etc.) do not touch this.
v1.fix_sketch: In the renderToString switch, give CellRole.overlay its own case that writes emptyMark (or a dedicated image mark like '#') instead of falling through to continuation's no-op, preserving column geometry; leave continuation as no-write. Add a snapshot test with images: InlineImageSupport.placements pinning image-region width.

findings.title: Mutating tester.viewportSize (or glyphTier/colorMode/images) mid-test leaves the tree's MediaQuery permanently stale — layout runs at the new size while widgets see the old one, a state impossible in production
findings.failure_scenario: Test pumps a responsive widget, then follows the field's own doc ('Mutable so tests can grow / shrink the viewport mid-test', fleury_tester.dart:113-118): `tester.viewportSize = CellSize(120, 40); tester.pumpWidget(...); tester.render();`. Verified at HEAD: MediaQuery.sizeOf(context) still reports 80x24 while the render buffer (and hence the whole layout pass — BuildOwner.renderFrame lays out at buffer.size, framework.dart:1547) is 120x40. A widget branching on MediaQuery.sizeOf renders its compact branch into the wide buffer — the golden either fails confusingly or, worse, passes while production shows the wide branch (run_app.dart:1097 rebuilds the ambient MediaQuery on every terminal resize, so this hybrid never occurs in a real app). Same silent no-op for `tester.glyphTier = GlyphTier.ascii` set after the first pumpWidget: the ASCII border fallback (basic.dart:851 reads MediaQuery.glyphTierOf) never engages.
findings.evidence: _wrap() (fleury_tester.dart:760-792) bakes viewportSize/colorMode/glyphTier/images into a MediaQueryData once, and _wrap is only invoked on the FIRST pumpWidget (`if (_root == null) _root = _owner.mountRoot(_wrap());` lines 215-224); subsequent pumpWidget calls only markNeedsBuild the inner _userEntry, so the MediaQuery widget above it is never rebuilt with fresh values. render() (line 527) meanwhile sizes the CellBuffer from the mutated field, so layout and MediaQuery diverge. Confirmed empirically: MediaQuery=80x24, buffer=120x40 after mutation + re-pump.

v1.why: _wrap() (fleury_tester.dart:760-792) bakes viewportSize/colorMode/glyphTier/images into MediaQueryData once; _wrap runs only when _root == null (215-216), later pumpWidget calls only markNeedsBuild the inner _userEntry (222-223), and the fields are plain hookless fields (113-126) whose doc invites mid-test mutation. render() (527) sizes the buffer from the mutated field and layout runs at buffer.size (framework.dart:1547 rootRender.layout(CellConstraints.loose(buffer.size))), so layout uses the new size while MediaQuery.sizeOf returns the stale 80x24 default (constructor line 74). Production stays in sync (run_app.dart ~1096-1098 resize rebuilds ambient MediaQuery). Existing tests mutate only before first pump (media_query_test.dart:17,24), pinning nothing.
v1.fix_sketch: Make the wrapper track the fields: convert viewportSize/colorMode/glyphTier/images to setters that mark the root (or a stateful wrapper owning the MediaQuery) dirty and flushBuild, or have pumpWidget/render() rebuild the MediaQuery data from current field values every call (e.g. wrap the tree in a Builder reading the tester fields so markNeedsBuild on the wrapper refreshes MediaQueryData).

findings.title: DiffView unified-diff parser misclassifies deletions/additions whose content starts with '--'/'++', corrupting paths, counts, line numbers, and hunk grouping
findings.failure_scenario: A git diff deletes the SQL comment line '-- drop old comment' (diff line: '--- drop old comment') inside a hunk. parseUnifiedDiff classifies it as a fileHeader and sets oldPath to 'drop old comment' for every subsequent row; the row renders in file-header style instead of red deletion style; deletionCount stays 0; oldCursor is not advanced so every later gutter line number in the hunk is off by one; currentHunkIndex is reset to null so hunk-mode copy (DiffViewCopyMode.hunk) from any later row exports a single line instead of the hunk, and semantic state reports selectedFilePath='drop old comment'. Symmetrically, an added line '++i;' (adding C code '++i;') is excluded by '!line.startsWith("+++")' and falls through to metadata. Verified by executing parseUnifiedDiff at HEAD: output shows fileHeader/oldPath='drop old comment', metadata for '+++i;', additions=0 deletions=0, and context line numbered old=2/new=2 instead of 3/3.
findings.evidence: diff_view.dart:206 'if (line.startsWith("--- "))' and :213 'if (line.startsWith("+++ "))' run before the deletion/addition branches with no in-hunk state check, and :234 'line.startsWith("+") && !line.startsWith("+++")' / :241 '!line.startsWith("---")' exclude legitimate content lines. The hunk regex at :657 captures the old/new line counts (groups 2 and 4) that would disambiguate header-vs-content, but :223-224 only parse groups 1 and 3 and the counts are never used. Fallthrough at :261 resets currentHunkIndex. Repro executed against HEAD via path-dep package.

v1.why: diff_view.dart:206 'startsWith("--- ")' runs before the deletion branch (:241) with no in-hunk tracking, so a deleted line '-- drop old comment' ('--- drop old comment') becomes a fileHeader: oldPath overwritten (:207), currentHunkIndex nulled (:209), deletionCount/oldCursor skipped (:243-245), later gutter numbers off by one, and _exportHunk returns one line when hunkIndex is null (:661). '+++i;' is excluded by :234 and falls to metadata (:261). Hunk regex :657 captures the disambiguating counts but :223-224 never use groups 2/4. No test pins this. Trigger needs col-0 '--'/'++' content, so P2 not P1.
v1.fix_sketch: Parse hunk counts (regex groups 2/4) and track remaining old/new lines; while inside a hunk classify '-'/'+' lines as deletion/addition unconditionally, and only recognize '---'/'+++' file headers when not inside a hunk.

findings.title: Sparkline, BarChart, Histogram, and Heatmap throw 'Unsupported operation: Infinity or NaN toInt' on a NaN data value, replacing the widget with an error box
findings.failure_scenario: Sparkline(data: [1, double.nan, 3]) — e.g. a metrics stream where one sample is 0/0 — computes t = (NaN - min)/range; both range guards are false for NaN so paint executes (t * 8).ceil() which throws UnsupportedError; RenderErrorBoundary contains it and paints the error presentation instead of the sparkline (a NaN as the newest/first value also poisons the autoscaled max, killing the whole strip). Same class verified in BarChart (bar_chart.dart:522 '((bar.value!.toDouble() / topVal) * chartRows * 8).round()' — one NaN bar also poisons autoscale topVal for all bars; _formatValue's v.truncate() throws too), Histogram (histogram.dart:137 '((d - lo) / binWidth).floor()' in build), and Heatmap (heatmap.dart:405 '(t * 4).ceil()'). LineChart explicitly documents double.nan as the framework's missing-data idiom, so feeding NaN to sibling chart widgets is expected usage; Sparkline's own semantics path (_maxFinite, line 101) already skips non-finite values, but paint does not.
findings.evidence: Executed at HEAD via test harness: 'Unsupported operation: Infinity or NaN toInt' at package:fleury_widgets/src/sparkline.dart 256:27 RenderSparkline.paint, bar_chart.dart 522:70 RenderBarChart._paintSingleBar, heatmap.dart 405:30 RenderHeatmap.paint (all contained by RenderErrorBoundary.paint at render_error_boundary.dart:201, which swaps in the error presentation). Dart's double.ceil()/round()/floor()/truncate() throw on NaN; the range guards (t <= 0 / t >= 1, d < lo || d > hi) are all false for NaN so it reaches the conversion. Gauge is safe only by accident (NaN.clamp(0,1) returns 1.0 on the VM).

v1.why: RenderSparkline.paint sparkline.dart:249-256: NaN v gives t=NaN, both guards (t<=0/t>=1) false, (t*8).ceil() throws UnsupportedError (Dart NaN.ceil). Same unguarded pattern: bar_chart.dart:493->522 ((NaN/topVal)*rows*8).round() with no finite filter (topVal<=0 guard also NaN-false), histogram.dart:135-137 (NaN passes d<lo||d>hi), heatmap.dart:401-405. No constructor asserts. line_chart.dart:169 documents double.nan as the missing-data idiom and guards isFinite itself, so NaN input is plausible usage. render_error_boundary.dart paint catch contains it, swapping the route/overlay-root subtree for the error presentation — degraded UI, not a crash.
v1.fix_sketch: Skip non-finite values in each paint/bucket loop (level 0 / continue, as line_chart does at :821/:828) and exclude non-finite values from autoscale min/max.

findings.title: LineChart with explicit xRange/yRange deletes entire segments touching finite out-of-range points (data vanishes instead of clipping) and can hang painting on large overshoots
findings.failure_scenario: LineChart(yRange: (0,100), series: [LineSeries([(0,50),(1,50),(2,150),(3,50),(4,50)])]) — a dashboard zoomed to 0..100 with a spike to 150: toPy maps y=150 to a negative pixel row, and _drawSegmentSolid's 'px0 < 0 || py0 < 0 || px1 < 0 || py1 < 0' skip (meant for the NaN sentinel) drops BOTH segments touching the spike, including their in-range halves. Verified render: '⠉⠉⠉⠉⠉⠁........⠈⠉⠉⠉⠉⠉' — a hole exactly where the peak should clip at the plot top, indistinguishable from the documented NaN missing-data gap. Overshoot on the other side maps to large positive pixels which ARE drawn: Bresenham walks every step, verified 209ms for one paint with one point 5e5x past xRange; the cost is linear in overshoot and per frame, so epoch-ms x data against a small explicit range (unit mistake) freezes the app for minutes.
findings.evidence: line_chart.dart:819-831 toPx/toPy return -1 only for non-finite inputs but never clamp finite out-of-range values, so above-range y / below-range x produce negative pixels conflated with the NaN sentinel checked at :1060, while below-range y / above-range x produce arbitrarily large positive pixels fed to BrailleBuffer.drawLine (braille.dart:56-77), whose loop runs once per pixel step with no bound. Both repros executed at HEAD (rendered hole + 209ms stopwatch). Peer chart libraries clip segments at the plot boundary instead.

v1.why: line_chart.dart:819-831 toPx/toPy return -1 only for non-finite; explicit ranges are literal (:796-797) and the segment loop :908-925 has no range filter. y>ymax maps to negative py, so _drawSegmentSolid:1060 (the NaN-sentinel check) drops both segments touching a spike, including in-range halves — data hole where clipping is expected. Opposite overshoot maps to huge positive pixels; braille.dart:55-76 drawLine is an unbounded per-pixel Bresenham while(true) (setPixel clips but the walk doesn't), so per-frame cost is linear in overshoot — epoch-ms x vs small range effectively freezes paint. Fill path :1074-1080 same.
v1.fix_sketch: Clip segments parametrically at the plot rect (Liang-Barsky/Cohen-Sutherland) before rasterizing; reserve the -1 sentinel for non-finite only, and bound drawLine iteration to the buffer extent as a backstop.

findings.title: CalendarHeatmap date math breaks across DST fall-back: boundary day renders on two rows, all later dates shift one weekday row, final day is dropped
findings.failure_scenario: CalendarHeatmap(start: DateTime(2026,10,25), end: DateTime(2026,11,7), values: {...}) rendered on a machine in America/New_York (or any DST timezone, range spanning the autumn transition — e.g. a 'last 90 days' activity graph in Oct-Dec): paint iterates days as weekStart.add(Duration(days: d)), which is absolute 24h arithmetic; after Nov 1 (25h day) every computed 'day' lands at 23:00 of the previous calendar date. Verified: row d=0 and d=1 of that week both resolve (via the _midnight fallback lookup) to Nov 1 — the value renders twice on Sunday AND Monday rows — Nov 2..6 each render one weekday row low, and Nov 7 (the range end) is never painted at all (data silently missing). _calendarDayCount/_weekCount use difference().inDays which truncates the 23h spring-forward day, so a 2-day range across spring-forward also reports 1 day.
findings.evidence: calendar_heatmap.dart:529-531 'weekStart = firstAnchor.add(Duration(days: w*7)); day = weekStart.add(Duration(days: d))' plus :533 fallback '_values[_midnight(day)]' snaps the 23:00 wall time to the previous date; :199-202 _calendarDayCount uses end.difference(start).inDays (truncating). Verified with TZ=America/New_York script at HEAD: w=1 d=1 wall=2026-11-01 23:00 → midnight-date 11/1 (duplicate), d=2 → 11/2 (should be 11/3 position), and 11/7 never produced. Fix is calendar-day iteration (DateTime(y,m,day+n)), not Duration addition.

v1.why: calendar_heatmap.dart:528-531 iterates days via local-DateTime add(Duration(days:n)) — absolute 24h; Dart documents wall-clock shift across DST. In a fall-back week (US Nov 1 2026), d=1 = anchor+24h = Nov 1 23:00: exact _values[day] misses (keys midnight-normalized :153-156) and the :533 _midnight fallback (:151) re-renders Nov 1 on the d=1 row (duplicate; d=0 hits Nov 1 directly); later days sit one row low; range-end day computes as end+23h, fails isAfter(_end) at :532 and is silently dropped. :199-202 difference().inDays truncates the 23h spring-forward day. Docs (:55) promise calendar-day bucketing with local-DateTime examples, so no UTC-contract refutation.
v1.fix_sketch: Iterate by calendar-date construction — DateTime(anchor.year, anchor.month, anchor.day + n) — instead of Duration addition, and compute day/week counts from date components.

findings.title: MarkdownDocument.plainText replaces every bold/italic/code/strikethrough span with the literal string '$1', corrupting semantic labels
findings.failure_scenario: MarkdownView(markdown: '# Deploy **failed** on `prod`') — parseMarkdownDocument builds block.plainText via _plainInlineText, which calls text.replaceAll(RegExp(r'`([^`]+)`'), r'$1') etc.; Dart's String.replaceAll performs NO group substitution (unlike JS), so the replacement is the literal two characters '$1'. Verified at HEAD: plainText == 'Deploy $1 on $1'. plainText is the Semantics label of every MarkdownView block (markdown_text.dart:839 'label: block.plainText') and a public MarkdownBlock field, so screen readers, agent-driven semantic consumers (fleury's core pillar), and the serve semantics wire all receive '$1' in place of the actual emphasized/code text for any block using inline markup.
findings.evidence: markdown_text.dart:1321-1326: four .replaceAll(RegExp(...), r'$1') calls; Dart API docs — replaceAll's replacement string is used literally (group refs require replaceAllMapped, which the link rule directly above at :1315 correctly uses, showing the intent). Executed repro at HEAD printed plain="Deploy $1 on $1" / "Use $1 here" while displayText stayed correct, confirming only the semantic/plain-text surface is corrupted.

v1.why: markdown_text.dart:1321-1326: four .replaceAll(RegExp(...), r'$1') calls; Dart String.replaceAll uses the replacement literally (group refs require replaceAllMapped — which the link rule at :1315 correctly uses), so every code/bold/strike/italic span becomes the literal '$1'. _plainInlineText feeds plainText for heading/paragraph/quote/listItem blocks (:417-482), which is the public MarkdownBlock.plainText field and the Semantics label at :839 — screen readers and semantic-wire consumers get 'Deploy $1 on $1'. Display text and value: sourceText (:840) stay correct, so impact is the semantic surface only — P2.
v1.fix_sketch: Change the four calls to replaceAllMapped(regex, (m) => m.group(1)!), matching the link rule at :1315.

findings.title: Lazy ListView renders permanently blank after a controller swap that doesn't change itemCount
findings.failure_scenario: App swaps the external controller on a builder-mode list while the item count happens to be unchanged -- e.g. per-conversation MessageListController/LogRegionController instances with equal counts, toggling `controller: c` <-> null, or (common misuse) recreating the controller in build(). The list unmounts every row and renders empty (verified: frame all-blank, visibleRange=null, controller.itemCount=0) and STAYS blank until some later change to itemCount; keyboard nav is also dead because the swapped-in controller never gets the initState-style default selection.
findings.evidence: `_ListViewState.didUpdateWidget` (list_view.dart:468-492) attaches the new controller but only pushes the count when `newCount != oldWidget.effectiveItemCount` (line 487), so a fresh controller keeps `_itemCount == 0`. `_RenderLazyListView.performLayout` reads `_controller._itemCount` (line 1272) and its `count == 0` branch (lines 1274-1279) unmounts all children and returns without ever writing the real count back -- unlike the eager `_RenderListView`, which self-heals from `_children.length` (line 876/920). Confirmed by harness probe: pumping the same 10-item ListView.builder with a second ListController renders an empty frame.

v1.why: Traced: _ListViewState.didUpdateWidget attaches swapped controller without initState's _itemCount push (list_view.dart:470-479 vs :448); count push gated on newCount != oldWidget.effectiveItemCount (:487), skipped when equal; fresh controller _itemCount==0 (:73). _LazyListBody.updateRenderObject (:1040) swaps it in; performLayout reads count 0 (:1272) -> empty branch (:1274-1279) unmounts all, visibleRange=null, never writes true count -> blank until a real itemCount change. Eager path self-heals from _children.length (:868, :920). Keyboard dead: null selection -> arrows ignored (:507), no didUpdateWidget defaulting. No controller-swap test exists.
v1.fix_sketch: After swapping controllers in didUpdateWidget, replay the initState attach: push _controller._itemCount = widget.effectiveItemCount, default/clamp the selection, and compare the count-push guard against the new controller's count instead of oldWidget's.

findings.title: An oversized set_value/press_key payload is misread as "app exited": the bridge tears the session down and SIGTERMs the healthy app, with no error to the agent
findings.failure_scenario: App healthy, agent calls set_value on a textField with a string of ~175k escape-heavy chars (e.g. '' or ESC repeated 175,000x — under the 200,000-char cap at mcp_server.dart:916, and the 'string' schema in value_schema.dart accepts any content). encodeSemanticAction JSON-encodes the value (remote_codec.dart:1166), inflating each control char to 6 bytes (~1.05 MB > the 1 MiB inputEvent/semanticAction payload cap, remote_protocol.dart:156). encodeFrame throws RemoteProtocolException synchronously inside transport.send (unix_socket_transport.dart:125); FleuryAppBridge._send's catch-all (app_bridge.dart:326-327) calls _markExited() — falsely declaring the app dead. bridge.done then fires bin/fleury_mcp.dart:131's finish(0), which bridge.close()→app.dispose()→SIGTERM/SIGKILLs the perfectly healthy app subprocess and exit(0)s the MCP server, usually before the tool response (a non-error 'changed:false') is flushed. One bad tool argument kills the app and the whole MCP session with zero error surfaced. Same path via press_key: {key: <1 MiB string>, modifiers:['ctrl']} — _toolPressKey has NO length bound on key at all (mcp_server.dart:1267-1318) and w.str(char) is uncapped (remote_codec.dart:1034).
findings.evidence: mcp_server.dart:913-916 claims _maxInputChars 'bounds a pathological payload below the wire's frame cap' — false once jsonEncode escaping (6x for control chars) is applied in encodeSemanticAction's uncapped w.vstr(jsonEncode(value)) (remote_codec.dart:1153-1167). encodeFrame throws when payload > remoteFramePayloadLimit (remote_protocol.dart:483-489; inputEvent/semanticAction limit = 1 MiB, line 156). transport.send encodes synchronously (unix_socket_transport.dart:123-126), so the throw lands in _send's `catch (_) { _markExited(); }` (app_bridge.dart:322-329) which is documented as the broken-socket path but also swallows per-frame encode rejections whose message literally says 'frame was not encoded' (connection intact). _markExited completes _exited → bin/fleury_mcp.dart:131 `bridge.done.then((_) => finish(0))` → bridge.close() → app.dispose() → killProcess SIGTERM→SIGKILL (spawn.dart:165-176) → exit(0). Contrast: the serve-side driver treats oversized encodes as recoverable (size-checks before send and resets the encoder, remote_driver.dart:356-374).

v1.why: Traced full chain at HEAD: _toolSetValue accepts <=200k chars (mcp_server.dart:1158; string schema unbounded, value_schema.dart:50,100-102) -> bridge.setValue -> _send -> transport.send calls encodeFrame synchronously (unix_socket_transport.dart:125) -> encodeSemanticAction's uncapped w.vstr(jsonEncode(value)) (remote_codec.dart:1166). Ran a Dart script: 175k U+0001 chars jsonEncode to 1,050,002 bytes > 1 MiB semanticAction cap (remote_protocol.dart:156,179-182) -> throw (483-489) -> catch(_){_markExited()} (app_bridge.dart:326-328) -> done -> fleury_mcp.dart:131 finish(0) -> bridge.close -> app.dispose SIGTERM/SIGKILL (spawn.dart:165-171) -> exit(0); tool handler still blocked 2s on status timeout so no response flushed. press_key chord path equally unbounded (mcp_server.dart:1267-1318, remote_codec.dart:1034); stdin LineSplitter uncapped (mcp_server.dart:85).
v1.fix_sketch: In FleuryAppBridge._send, catch RemoteProtocolException separately (frame-not-encoded means connection intact) and surface it as a tool error instead of _markExited — or pre-check encoded size before send the way remote_driver.presentSemantics does (remote_driver.dart:353-364). Additionally bound jsonEncode(value) bytes in _toolSetValue and cap press_key char length.

findings.title: SEMANTIC_ACTION_RESULT is matched to the pending mutation without checking the frame's id/action — a late result from a previous slow action is attributed to the next invoke_action/set_value, and that call's real result is dropped
findings.failure_scenario: App has a Semantics(onAction:) handler on node A that returns a Future taking >2s (the framework awaits async handlers before sending the result: semantics.dart:1395-1401 `await callback(action)`, run_app.dart ~1020 `await invokeSemanticActionFromElement` then presentSemanticActionResult). Agent: invoke_action(A) → the bridge's status wait times out at 2s (_expectActionResult's `.timeout(2s, onTimeout: () => null)`, app_bridge.dart:260-264) and the tool returns without status, leaving A's completer armed. Agent then calls invoke_action(B) (or set_value) → _expectActionResult completes A's stale completer with null and arms B's. At t≈5s the app finishes A and sends SemanticActionResultFrame(A, activate, failed); _onFrame (app_bridge.dart:451-457) completes B's completer with A's status without comparing f.id/f.action. B's tool call then throws _ToolFailure 'The app's handler for "activate" on "B" threw. This is an app-side bug…' (mcp_server.dart:1046-1053) — even though B's action is still queued app-side (run_app's per-connection semanticActionTail) and hasn't run. When B's real result arrives, _pendingActionResult is already null and it is silently dropped. Inverse mix-up too: A completed / B actually disabled → B falsely reports 'completed'.
findings.evidence: The frame carries the correlation data — SemanticActionResultFrame(this.id, this.action, this.status) (remote_protocol.dart:410-411) and the app echoes the request's id+action (remote_driver.dart:378-385) — but app_bridge.dart:451-457 completes _pendingActionResult with f.status unconditionally, and _expectActionResult (app_bridge.dart:253-264) takes id/action parameters it never uses. The comment 'Mutations are serialized by the MCP server, so at most one is pending' misses that the 2s timeout de-arms the WAIT but not the app-side work: a result can arrive after its own tool call returned and while the NEXT mutation is armed. Compare queryDebug (app_bridge.dart:276-292), which correctly correlates by seq. No test sends SemanticActionResultFrame at all (grep of packages/fleury_mcp/test/).

v1.why: _expectActionResult takes id/action but never stores them (app_bridge.dart:253-264); its 2s timeout resolves the caller null while leaving _pendingActionResult armed; _onFrame completes the pending completer with f.status with no f.id/f.action check (app_bridge.dart:451-457) though the frame carries both (remote_protocol.dart SemanticActionResultFrame; echoed at remote_driver.dart:379-385). App side genuinely delays: await callback (semantics.dart:1399,1413) inside the serialized semanticActionTail (run_app.dart:953,998-1032), so with a >2s async handler on A, invoke_action(A) returns statusless, invoke_action(B) arms a fresh completer, A's late failed result completes B's -> _ToolFailure blaming B (mcp_server.dart:1046-1052) while B is still queued; B's real result finds _pendingActionResult null and drops. No test sends SemanticActionResultFrame (grep packages/fleury_mcp/test/ = 0 hits).
v1.fix_sketch: Store the armed (id, action) with the pending completer; in _onFrame complete it only when f.id and f.action match (ignore stale mismatches), and null out _pendingActionResult when the 2s timeout fires so a late result can't bind to the next mutation. Mirror queryDebug's seq-correlation pattern (app_bridge.dart:276-292).

findings.title: Keystroke backlog (including Ctrl+C) silently discarded after an output-stall thaw on terminals that never answer the DA probe — scripted/CI PTY sessions become uncontrollable
findings.failure_scenario: Environment: any PTY host that does not answer the startup Device-Attributes query and does not continuously drain output — expect scripts, `script`, CI recorders, agent harnesses that send keys then sleep (real terminal emulators answer DA and always drain, so interactive use is unaffected). Sequence: (1) startup image/width probes time out unanswered and arm _drainingLateProbe with a 250ms grace timer; (2) the app's synchronous stdout writes fill the un-drained PTY buffer and freeze the isolate before/while the grace timer is pending (any animating app — all four samples and the storybook caret blink — fills ~32KB in seconds); (3) every byte typed during the frozen stretch is queued by the kernel; (4) on thaw, Dart delivers the queued stdin chunk first — while _drainingLateProbe is still true — diverting it into _probeBuffer, then the long-expired grace timer fires _giveUpLateProbeDrain and discards it all. Result: every key sent while the session was stalled, including Ctrl+C, silently vanishes; combined with the dead 'q' key (finding 1) a scripted session cannot exit the demo at all (kill -9 required). Reproduced deterministically: storybook/agent/dashboard all 'hang' when keys are sent during non-drained periods; same runs succeed with FLEURY_IMAGE_PROBE=0 + FLEURY_AMBIGUOUS_WIDTH=off, with drain cycles between sends, or with DA answered.
findings.evidence: posix_driver.dart:472-493 (_replayPostProbeInput: no DA terminator -> _drainingLateProbe=true + Timer(lateProbeGrace=250ms)); :348-366 (stdin listener diverts all bytes to _probeBuffer while draining); :510-518 (_giveUpLateProbeDrain discards the buffer — its own comment admits 'Real input typed during the grace is dropped too, but the window is bounded'; the window is NOT bounded: the timer callback runs after the queued stdin delivery when the event loop was stalled, so the 'grace' spans the whole stall). Empirical bisect at HEAD: probes on + no DA + send-during-sleep -> onEvent log stays empty and app never exits (instrumented bins logged zero events while SIGWINCH-driven ResizeEvents still flowed, proving only the parsed-stdin path was affected); probes disabled -> same app receives TextInputEvent('x') and exits on Ctrl+C; drain-cycled sends (thaw before each key) -> exits; DA/CPR answered like a real terminal -> exits.

v1.why: Traced: probe timeout with no DA -> _replayPostProbeInput arms _drainingLateProbe + 250ms Timer (posix_driver.dart:491-492, grace :134); listener diverts all stdin to _probeBuffer while draining (:352-356); _giveUpLateProbeDrain discards (:514-518). Under an output-stall (sync stdout write to a full un-drained PTY blocks the isolate past the deadline), Dart's FIFO isolate queue delivers a stdin-readable that arrived pre-deadline BEFORE the expired timer message; that one read drains every keystroke typed during the entire stall (incl. Ctrl+C) into the buffer, then the timer discards it — so the 'bounded' window (:512-513 comment) is not bounded. Keys typed during the probe window itself are also kept-then-discarded (:470-471). Interactive terminals answer DA and drain, so unaffected — edge environment, P2.
v1.fix_sketch: In the draining branch of the stdin listener, check the grace deadline against a monotonic clock at delivery time: if already expired, run _giveUpLateProbeDrain-style scan first (strip a recognizable DA/partial-CSI prefix if present) and replay the remainder to the parser instead of buffering-then-discarding. Alternatively, on give-up, replay any tail that does not look like a probe reply rather than discarding wholesale.

findings.title: FrameSemanticsPipeline.dispose() strands every pending awaitIdle() future when a deferred flush is outstanding — permanent hang despite the documented dispose-completes-idle contract
findings.failure_scenario: Embed host (fleury_web MountedApp, default TimerSemanticFlushScheduler per run_tui_surface.dart:193-195): a frame is presented with semantic work pending → `onFramePresented` sets `_flushScheduled = true` and schedules the deferred flush for the next macrotask (frame_semantics_pipeline.dart:150-155). A caller invokes `host.awaitSemanticIdle()` → since `_flushScheduled` is true this returns a pending `_idleCompleter.future` (196-199) — the ONLY state in which awaitIdle returns a pending future. Before the timer fires, teardown runs `host.dispose()` → `semanticsPipeline.dispose()` (372-376): `_disposed = true; _scheduler.dispose(); _completeIdleIfQuiet();`. The scheduler dispose cancels the pending flush (TimerSemanticFlushScheduler.dispose cancels `_pending`, semantic_flush_scheduler.dart:57-61; MicrotaskSemanticFlushScheduler's already-queued microtask early-returns on `_disposed`, 77-81), so `flushNow` never runs and `_flushScheduled` never clears. `_completeIdleIfQuiet()` (201-206) then early-returns on `if (_flushScheduled) return;` without completing the completer. The awaitIdle future never resolves → `await host.awaitSemanticIdle()` (used across fleury_web embed tests and available to any embedder via MountedApp.awaitSemanticIdle) hangs forever. run_tui_surface.dart:588-591 explicitly documents the opposite contract: 'Also completes any outstanding awaitIdle() so a disposed host can't leave a test (or caller) hanging on a cancelled flush.'
findings.evidence: frame_semantics_pipeline.dart:196-199 `awaitIdle()` returns a pending future only while `_flushScheduled` is true; 201-206 `_completeIdleIfQuiet()` refuses to complete while `_flushScheduled` is true; 372-376 `dispose()` never clears `_flushScheduled` before calling it, and disposes the scheduler so the one callback that would clear the flag (`_runScheduledFlush` → `flushNow`, 180-224) is cancelled — dispose's `_completeIdleIfQuiet()` call is therefore unreachable-effective (any live `_idleCompleter` implies `_flushScheduled == true`). Both schedulers drop the pending flush on dispose (semantic_flush_scheduler.dart:57-61 and 77-81). Consumed via MountedApp.awaitSemanticIdle (packages/fleury_web/lib/src/run_tui_surface.dart:532-533) whose dispose path (`_disposeHostResourcesBestEffort`, 588-591) claims dispose completes outstanding awaitIdle. Fix shape: set `_flushScheduled = false` in dispose before `_completeIdleIfQuiet()`.
also: lens-lifecycle: FrameSemanticsPipeline.dispose strands outstanding awaitIdle() futures when a deferred flush is scheduled — contradicting its documented dispose contract

v1.why: Traced: onFramePresented sets _flushScheduled=true (frame_semantics_pipeline.dart:151); awaitIdle returns a pending completer only in that state (:197-198); dispose (:372-376) never clears the flag, and _scheduler.dispose() cancels the one callback that would (Timer cancelled, semantic_flush_scheduler.dart:57-61; microtask early-returns on _disposed, :79) — so _completeIdleIfQuiet (:202) early-returns forever and the future hangs. MountedApp.dispose calls pipeline.dispose with no flush-first (run_tui_surface.dart:589-592) directly under the comment promising dispose completes outstanding awaitIdle. flushPendingNow callers (run_app.dart:1016, run_tui_surface.dart:360) are stopped pre-dispose; no test pins this; file unchanged since 58252d1. Teardown-race edge case → P2.
v1.fix_sketch: In FrameSemanticsPipeline.dispose(), set _flushScheduled = false before calling _completeIdleIfQuiet() (mirroring flushNow's disposed path at :214-217): `_disposed = true; _flushScheduled = false; _scheduler.dispose(); _completeIdleIfQuiet();`

findings.title: Unterminated bracketed paste now captures ALL input forever (incl. Ctrl+C escape hatch) — idle-flush recovery removed with no fallback deadline
findings.failure_scenario: App running on a live TTY; the paste source dies mid-bracketed-paste while the keyboard stays alive (tmux paste-buffer/SSH multiplexed stream interrupted after ESC[200~, terminator ESC[201~ never arrives). Every subsequent keystroke — arrows, Enter, debug-shell hotkeys, and Ctrl+C (raw mode disables ISIG, so 0x03 is just a paste byte) — is appended to the never-ending paste and streamed into the focused widget as paste segments. The user cannot interact with or exit the app from the keyboard; only an external kill/SIGTERM or stdin EOF recovers. Before PR #109 (8e94c26, in this audit window), the driver's ~30ms idle flush emitted the pending paste and returned the parser to ground, so the session stayed controllable.
findings.evidence: flush() at packages/fleury/lib/src/terminal/input_parser.dart:160-166 now does `case _State.paste: break;` (deliberate per its comment: don't split a slow SSH paste into typed keys — the old behavior was itself a paste-to-keystroke injection hazard, so the fix direction is sound). But there is no inactivity deadline of any kind: only the exact ESC[201~ terminator (_finishPaste via _consume) or stream EOF (finish(), posix_driver.dart:380) leaves paste state. The runtime escape tier cannot fire either: Ctrl+C exit and tryConsumeDebugKey/tryConsumeDebugText in packages/fleury/lib/src/runtime/run_app.dart handleDriverEvent operate on parsed KeyEvent/TextInputEvents, which the parser never emits while consuming paste content (_appendPasteByte, input_parser.dart:711). Mitigation kernel: a generous (e.g. 5-10s) paste-inactivity deadline calling _finishPaste, distinct from the 30ms ESC debounce, would preserve the security fix while restoring recoverability.

v1.why: Traced: paste state entered via DEC 2004 (terminal_sequences.dart:16); only exits are full ESC[201~ (input_parser.dart:690-692) or finish() from stdin onDone (posix_driver.dart:380). The sole idle timer (30ms, posix_driver.dart:913) hits 'case _State.paste: break' (input_parser.dart:160-164). cfmakeraw disables ISIG (posix_driver.dart:565,933), so Ctrl+C=0x03 is appended as paste content (input_parser.dart:707,711); no KeyEvent ever reaches run_app's Ctrl+C exit (run_app.dart:630-637) or debug hotkeys (608-616). Pre-8e94c26 _flushPaste restored _state=ground on idle; removed with no replacement deadline. Edge-case trigger but total keyboard lockout incl. escape hatch: P2, not P3.
v1.fix_sketch: Add a paste-inactivity deadline distinct from the 30ms ESC debounce: when the parser is mid-paste and no bytes arrive for a generous window (e.g. 5-10s), the driver calls a new parser method that finalizes the pending paste exactly like _finishPaste (emit accumulated bytes as PasteEvent, return to ground). Short SSH pauses stay far under the deadline so the injection fix is preserved, while an abandoned paste recovers keyboard control.

findings.title: Post-mount startup-event-buffer overflow leaves runApp's future permanently unresolved and the error silently swallowed
findings.failure_scenario: App mounts normally; more than 4096 events (or >1 MiB of event payload, e.g. one large paste) are buffered into _StartupEventBuffer after enter()'s pre-mount check but before activateDriverEvents() finishes — realistic during the `await HotReloadController.attach` window on the native path (hot reload is on by default), and deterministic when a synchronous custom-driver stream emits recursively from an app onEvent during the startup replay. activateDriverEvents() throws the overflow StateError (line 676/684) inside the zone body's try; the finally runs cleanup() (terminal restored, errorReporter disposed), then the error reaches the runZonedGuarded handler — which calls errorReporter.report (no-op: reporter already disposed, so not even a stderr line) and then takes the NON-fatal branch because frameDriver.rootElement is non-null, never completing `done`. runApp's returned future never settles: the caller's `await runApp(...)` hangs, its post-exit shutdown/exit-code logic never runs (the process either exits 0 silently when the event loop drains or hangs forever if the app holds other resources). Verified by repro: driver.restore() ran, then 3s later the future was still pending with zero diagnostics emitted — the exact 'fail startup loudly' path the buffer was built for fails silently instead.
findings.evidence: run_app.dart:676/684 throw the overflow from activateDriverEvents(), which is called at line 1260 AFTER driver.mountRoot (line 1236) inside `try { ... } finally { await cleanup(); }`; the success-path `done.complete(appExit)` at line 1266 is skipped by the throw, and the zone guard's fatal gate (lines 1297-1301: reportingFailed || renderUnrecoverable || rootElement == null || isStorming) evaluates false because FrameDriver.dispose (frame_driver.dart:493) never clears _rootElement — so no branch ever completes `done` (returned at line 1312). RuntimeErrorReporter.report (runtime_error_overlay.dart:74) returns before onLog when disposed, making the error fully silent. Existing tests only cover the DURING-enter overflow (run_app_test.dart:399/422), which throws pre-mount where rootElement==null makes the gate correctly fatal. Reproduced at HEAD: scratchpad/overflow_hang_repro.dart exits 42 with 'runApp's future is still pending and no error was surfaced anywhere'.

v1.why: Traced at HEAD: events buffer while driverEventsReady==false (run_app.dart:884); pre-mount check (907) only covers enter(). Post-mount window exists: `await HotReloadController.attach` (1245, enableHotReload default true at 244/330) sits between mountRoot (1236) and activateDriverEvents (1260), whose throw (676/684) skips done.complete (1266), runs cleanup (1264) disposing the reporter (765), then hits the guard: report() no-ops before onLog when disposed (runtime_error_overlay.dart:74), FrameDriver.dispose never clears _rootElement (frame_driver.dart:493-496), so the fatal gate (1297-1301) is all-false and done.future (1312) never settles, error fully silent. Existing tests (run_app_test.dart:399/422) throw pre-mount where frameDriver==null makes the gate fatal.
v1.fix_sketch: Make the post-mount startup overflow take the fatal path: add `disposed` (cleanup already ran) to the zone guard's fatal gate at run_app.dart:1298, or catch the activateDriverEvents throw in the body and `done.completeError` after the finally (mirroring the pre-mount behavior); alternatively clear _rootElement in FrameDriver.dispose. Add a test injecting >4096 events during a stubbed HotReloadController.attach (or a synchronous recursive driver during replay) asserting runApp's future completes with the StateError.

findings.title: Duplicate GlobalKey with one occurrence nested under a new sibling bypasses every duplicate check: the same Element is committed at two tree positions (silent corruption instead of the designed loud error)
findings.failure_scenario: Frame 1: Row(children: [KeyedW(key: gk), Text('x')]). Frame 2 (user error — key used twice): Row(children: [WrapB(child: KeyedW(key: gk)), KeyedW(key: gk)]). Slot 0's inflate steals the old keyed element into WrapB (claims gk). Slot 1 then matches the SAME element out of keyedOlds via keyedOlds.remove(gk) + canUpdate — this match path never calls _claimGlobalKey and never checks that the candidate's _parent is still this element — so result[1] is an element whose _parent is WrapB's subtree. Verified: no error is thrown and toStringDeep shows the identical StatefulElement printed under both WrapB and directly under Row. Consequences: _syncChildRenderObjects collects the same RenderObject twice into replaceAllChildren, depth/parent bookkeeping is wrong for one path, and a later Row unmount visits the element twice (double dispose). The sibling pre-check at :1799-1808 only scans top-level keys, and GlobalKey._register is never reached because no new element mounts — so the release-safe duplicate detection added in PR #103 is fully bypassed and the framework corrupts instead of throwing.
findings.evidence: packages/fleury/lib/src/widgets/framework.dart:1855-1863 — keyed match path (`keyedOlds.remove(newKey)` -> canUpdate -> matched) trusts that the map entry is still this element's child; a mid-loop steal (forgetChild :2001 removes it from _children but not from keyedOlds) invalidates that. No `identical(candidate._parent, this)` check and no _claimGlobalKey call on this path (claims happen only in inflateWidget :889). Repro output shows the same element instance in two positions with zero errors in both assert and release modes.

v1.why: Reproduced at HEAD. Frame 2 Row[Wrap(KeyedW(gk)), KeyedW(gk)]: slot 0's inflate steals the element into Wrap (claims gk once); slot 1's keyed match path :1855-1863 pulls the SAME element from stale keyedOlds (forgetChild :2001 never updates it), with no identical(candidate._parent, this) check and no _claimGlobalKey on the match path (claims only in inflateWidget :889); the :1799-1808 pre-check scans only direct-child keys. Repro: pump error=null (silent — the designed loud duplicate error bypassed), element committed at two positions; teardown showed the corruption: dispose x2, ':1064 dropChild called on the wrong parent', assert :625, 'Element has no owner'. Requires user error (duplicate key) -> P3.
v1.fix_sketch: In the keyed match path, verify identical(candidate._parent, this) (stale after a mid-loop steal) — on mismatch treat as no-match and inflate, which re-claims and throws the designed duplicate-GlobalKey StateError. Shares the forgotten-children fix with finding 0.

findings.title: ChangeNotifier invokes listeners that were removed during the same notifyListeners pass, breaking the Listenable contract and enabling use-after-dispose callbacks
findings.failure_scenario: Two listeners on one notifier; listener A's reaction tears down the thing listener B belongs to (removes B and disposes its resources — e.g. a selection/focus listener that cancels a peer subscription, or disposes a controller whose listener closure is B). notifyListeners iterates a pre-made copy (List.of), so B is still invoked after its removal and executes against disposed state — e.g. addListener on the disposed object throws 'addListener called on disposed ChangeNotifier', or the closure mutates freed resources. Verified with a minimal repro: listener registered after a remover was still invoked (prints true) — Flutter's ChangeNotifier guarantees a listener removed during notification is not called (it nulls the entry in the live list), so code ported from Flutter that relies on remove-during-notify silently misbehaves. Same copy-iteration also keeps delivering to remaining listeners after a listener calls dispose() on the notifier mid-notification.
